# The Superpower Codices

The obsidian floor of the library was still wet with the faint, violet-tinged residue of Kaedan's vomit, the acrid scent mingling with the omnipresent ozone and ancient dust. He had not moved from the spot where his father had left him, the crate of Stat Journals now scattered around his feet like the discarded shells of some horrible, hatched creature. His throat was raw, his arcane core throbbing with the sympathetic aftershocks of the horrors he had absorbed, and yet a grim, resolute stillness had settled over him. He understood now. The Stat Journals had been the primer, the necessary foundation, teaching him that the King's raw attributes were each, individually, an infinite, unstoppable apocalypse. But, Kaedan realized, staring at the towering, mile-high shelves that stretched into the non-linear vanishing point of the archive, the Stat Journals were only the beginning.

The library was vast. The crate his father had brought was merely a curated selection, the most critical firsthand accounts. Beyond it, extending into the frozen, non-Euclidean depths of the archive, were millions more volumes. Histories, spell-scrolls, genealogical records, and, Kaedan now understood, entire codices dedicated to the superpowers. He turned, his legs stiff and aching from his prolonged vigil, and began to walk deeper into the library. The preservation runes on the petrified bone-shelves pulsed faintly as he passed, illuminating titles in languages that had not been spoken since before the rise of Thronegate.

Eventually, guided by an instinct he could not name—perhaps a whisper from the ancient blood of Thorin that flowed in his veins—he stopped before a section of shelving carved from what looked like solidified, screaming obsidian. The spines here were not leather or parchment. They were bound in materials that defied easy categorization: woven light, solidified probability, the crystallized tears of dead gods. A single, unadorned shelf bore three volumes, each separated by precisely the width of a human hand. Their spines were engraved with simple, silver numerals: *Year 350,000*, *Year 750,000*, *Year 900,000*. Superpower Journals.

Kaedan reached for the first.

---

## Year 350,000 — The Incident of Absolute Reality Restructuring

The volume was bound in what appeared to be woven, petrified lightning—frozen arcs of electrical discharge that still crackled faintly beneath Kaedan's trembling fingertips. The title page was inscribed with a name: *Arch-Weaver Castor, Ninth Court Wizard of Thronegate.* The handwriting that followed was erratic, the letters jagged and uneven, as if the author had been physically vibrating while writing. The ink was an unsettling shade of deep, dried amber, and Kaedan realized with a cold lurch that it was not ink at all, but the writer's own plasma, extracted directly from his veins and used as pigment on the page. The journal opened with a single, stark epigraph:

*The Final Molders believed they could reshape existence. The King showed them that the only thing reality can reshape is itself, and only at His command.*

The text that followed was a torrent, a desperate, vomiting flood of words that seemed to claw at the page:

I write this in my own humoral fluids because the traditional inks of the library have refused to touch my quill. The universe itself is still reeling, Castor, and its substance rebels against being recorded. I will try. I must try. The future of our lineage depends on knowing what happens when an enemy forces the King to demonstrate one of His apocalyptic superpowers. Not a stat. Not a passive attribute. A fully realized, conceptual superpower, evolved and re-evolved by the Weakness Paradox until it has become something utterly unrecognizable from its original genesis.

The Final Molders were, in their way, the most sympathetic opposition we have ever faced. They were a multi-dimensional civilization of reality architects, beings who had achieved mastery over the fundamental fabric of spacetime itself. They did not forge weapons or build armies. They simply learned to rewrite the script of existence, editing the source code of reality to manifest their will. When Thronegate's envoys arrived with the offer of unconditional surrender, the Molders did not refuse. They simply decided that the envoys had never existed. The edit was seamless, retroactive, perfect. Our diplomats were erased from the timeline with such precision that even my amplified arcane senses could detect no lingering trace.

The King, as always, knew. He had known for the entire hundred-thousand-year history of their civilization. He had allowed them to flourish, to perfect their craft, to reach the absolute zenith of reality manipulation, purely because He was curious. He possesses a superpower, you see, one of the billions that bloomed in His stasis and have been multiplying and evolving ever since. He calls it, with characteristic understatement, "Reality Restructuring." Its original function was simple enough: the ability to rewrite the fundamental laws and substance of any localized reality. But after one million years of the Weakness Paradox evolution, after being triggered infinite times by the arrogant thoughts of countless enemies who believed they could out-code the source code, the superpower had evolved. It had become something else. Something with layers.

The King decided to test it. He materialized in the central citadel of the Final Molders, appearing not as a conqueror but as a curious observer. His ruggedly handsome face was calm, His salt-and-pepper beard immaculate, His posture relaxed. He looked at the Molders—tall, shimmering beings of pure semantic energy who surrounded Him in a vast, crystalline amphitheater—and He simply said, "Show me what you can do."

The Molders, arrogant and certain, obliged. They activated their greatest construct, the Loom of Absolute Revision, a hyper-dimensional engine that could edit any aspect of reality, past, present, or future, applying changes that would ripple backward and forward through all timelines simultaneously. Their first edit was to rewrite the King's own existence. They attempted to insert a single, new line into the fundamental code of His being: Vulnerability equals absolute. Flesh becomes mortal. Death becomes possible.

The King felt the edit attempt to propagate through His quantum structure. He allowed it to touch Him. And then, He activated His superpower. Not fully. Never fully. He exercised an amount so infinitesimally, recursively fractional that calling it zero percent would be an obscene overestimation. If you divided the smallest conceivable unit of supernatural exertion by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity an infinite number of times, repeating this process for a duration equal to the total lifespan of all possible omniverses, the resulting fraction would still be too vast to accurately represent the amount of His superpower that He permitted to activate.

The Reality Restructuring superpower, in its evolved, paradox-enhanced state, did not merely counter the Molder's edit. It did not simply negate it, or deflect it, or overwrite it. The first evolved enhancement was Absolute Semantic Inversion. The superpower seized the edit and inverted its target vector. The "Vulnerability equals absolute" command was reflected back upon the Final Molders themselves, but magnified, twisted into a state of agonizing permanence. Their shimmering bodies, which had existed as pure semantic energy for eons, were violently, excruciatingly forced into absolute, mortal, agonizingly fragile flesh. They were given hearts that could stop. Lungs that could drown. Bones that could shatter.

But the horror had only begun. The second evolved enhancement was Recursive Conceptual Overwrite. The superpower did not merely apply the edit once. It took the inverted command and fed it into a recursive loop, applying it to the Molders over and over, each time slightly differently, each time more devastating. Their new flesh was mutated. It grew tumors that were conscious and screamed. Their bones, after shattering, reformed in impossible, jagged configurations that pierced their own organs from within. Their lungs filled with acid, then solidified into glass, then dissolved back into tissue, only to fill with acid again. The cycle repeated infinitely fast, an eternity of biological torment compressed into what, from my perspective, was only a single, wet, agonizing second of screaming.

The third evolved enhancement was the most terrifying of all: Narrative Primacy Enforcement. The superpower reached beyond the Molders themselves and edited the entire underlying narrative framework of their civilization. It rewrote their history, their culture, their entire species' existence, so that the state of eternal, agonizing flesh-purgatory they were now experiencing had always been their destiny. Every Molder who had ever lived, across all their timelines, was retroactively rewritten to have been born solely for the purpose of suffering this one, infinite moment of torment. Their proud history of reality authorship was erased and replaced with a single, eternal truth: they were created to be the King's cautionary tale.

The collateral damage, as you may now unfortunately expect, was cataclysmic. The Reality Restructuring superpower, even at its infinitely restrained near-zero activation, created a standing wave of narrative inversion that propagated outward through the semantic fabric of all adjacent realities. The concept of "stable physical law" was temporarily unraveled across a region encompassing approximately two hundred septillion multiverses. In those realms, gravity became a suggestion, time flowed sideways, and matter spontaneously transmuted into screaming, self-aware paradoxes. The death toll was, as always, beyond any number I can sanely record. Whole civilizations, entire species, and countless vibrant cultures were simply unmade as the basic rules that allowed their biology to function ceased to exist.

The King observed this cascade of absolute, ontological annihilation with a clinical detachment that I have come to recognize as His "testing" expression. He noted the collapse of the semantic fabric, the propagation of the inversion wave, the deletion of the septillion multiverses. He did not restore them immediately. He waited, watching the void, curious as to whether any spontaneous new reality would self-generate from the chaos. None did. After approximately four seconds, He grew bored. With a casual, effortless blink, He restored every single one of the two hundred septillion multiverses, perfectly, instantaneously, leaving no trace of their temporary non-existence. The Final Molders, however, were not restored to their original state. He left them as they were: screaming, eternally recycled mounds of agonized, impossible flesh, trapped in a recursive loop of biological torment that would persist until the last star in the omniverse died. He had created them for this purpose, after all. Their narrative now demanded it.

I, Castor, write this account with my own blood, because I have no tears left to weep. I have served my King faithfully for fifty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body collapses into the dust. But I have seen what happens when an enemy forces Him to use even the faintest shadow of a single superpower. The Final Molders thought they could edit reality. They learned that the only reality is His will, and that their role in His narrative had always been to suffer.

---

Kaedan's hands, still clutching the crackling, lightning-bound volume, were shaking so violently that the preserved arcs of electricity were beginning to scorch his fingertips. He barely felt the pain. His mind was trapped in the image of the Final Molders, their proud, semantic bodies twisted into screaming, recursive flesh—an eternity of biological torment designed by a God who had, with a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of His power, rewritten their entire species' purpose for existing. He closed the volume with a convulsive slam, the lightning arcs snapping at the air with an angry, electric hiss, and placed it back upon the shelf.

The second volume awaited.

---

## Year 750,000 — The Incident of Absolute Information Dissemination

This journal was markedly different in construction from its predecessor. It was bound not in lightning, but in what appeared to be countless layers of translucent, overlapping insect wings, each one the size of a human palm, their surfaces iridescent with trapped rainbows. The wings were not dead; they twitched faintly as Kaedan lifted the book, beating with a soft, whispering rhythm that sounded almost like a distant, muffled heartbeat. The inscription, embossed in what looked like frozen moonlight, read: *High Curator Septimus, Fifteenth Court Wizard of Thronegate.* The handwriting inside was achingly beautiful, a flowing, calligraphic script that looked more like an illuminated manuscript than a journal. But the beauty of the script was a lie; the content was as horrific as anything Kaedan had yet read.

The prologue read:

*The Skein of Whispered Truths believed that knowledge was a weapon. The King demonstrated that His knowledge is not merely comprehensive, but actively corrosive to any mind that attempts to contain even a counterfeit of it. Information, in His hands, is not power. It is a plague.*

The text unfurled with the grim, poetic cadence of a funeral dirge:

I write this account in my finest calligraphic hand, because the aesthetic beauty of the script is the only thing that keeps my mind anchored to the concept of sanity. The events I witnessed occurred three days ago, and the screaming—the endless, multi-layered, telepathic SCREAMING—has still not entirely faded from the background radiation of the palace's psychic wards.

The Skein of Whispered Truths were, by any rational measure, the most intelligent opposition Thronegate has ever encountered. They were a collective intelligence composed of trillions of individual minds, all linked into a single, vast, hyper-dimensional neural network that spanned the entire breadth of their native multiverse. Their processing capacity was so immense that they had long ago solved every mathematical, philosophical, and scientific problem their reality contained. They had achieved a state of nearly perfect knowledge, and they were, in their hubris, utterly convinced that the King's omniscience was a myth—a propaganda tool, a bluff, an intimidation tactic. Because true omniscience, they reasoned, was a logical impossibility; the observer effect alone, the fact that knowing a thing changes the thing, would create infinite recursive loops of self-referencing data that would crash any consciousness, no matter how powerful.

They were, in a technical sense, correct. For any finite mind. But the King's intelligence has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for three-quarters of a million years. His consciousness does not merely encompass all information; it defines what information is. The Skein, in their elaborate, mathematically flawless argument, had simply failed to account for infinity.

Their attack was not physical. It was an information assault of breathtaking elegance. They compiled the sum total of all knowledge their collective had ever accumulated—every secret, every mathematical theorem, every historical detail, every personal memory of every individual who had ever joined their network—and they compressed it into a single, hyper-dense packet of pure, cognitive overload. They called it the Epistemological Singularity. The theory was simple: they would transmit this packet directly into the King's mind, and the sheer, overwhelming volume of data would, at minimum, stun Him long enough for a secondary attack to incapacitate His physical form. At maximum, the logical paradoxes embedded within the data packet would trap His consciousness in an infinite, self-referencing loop from which He could never escape.

The King, possessing absolute omniscience, had been aware of every single thought that contributed to the Epistemological Singularity's construction. He had watched them compile it, debate it, refine it. He had allowed it because He was curious about one of His oldest and most evolved superpowers. He calls it, with a soft, almost paternal affection, "Information Dissemination." Its original function, born in the stasis event, was the ability to transmit information—any information, of any complexity—into any target, anywhere, bypassing all defenses and filters. After seven hundred and fifty thousand years of the Weakness Paradox evolution, after being triggered uncountable times by enemies who believed they could outthink Him, the superpower had evolved multiple layers of horrifying, unfair enhancements.

The Skein transmitted their Epistemological Singularity. It struck the King's passive psychic shields, which He had deactivated entirely for the test. It entered His consciousness. He absorbed it. He understood it. Every piece of data, every paradox, every memory. He assimilated it completely in exactly zero Planck seconds. Then He activated His superpower. The fraction He used was, as always, so infinitesimally, recursively near-zero that attempting to express it mathematically would require inventing an entirely new system of notation. If you multiplied the total number of Planck seconds that have ever elapsed in the entire history of the omniverse by itself an infinite number of times, the resulting number would still be too small to serve as the denominator for the fraction of His superpower that He permitted to activate.

The Information Dissemination superpower, in its evolved state, did not merely transmit a counter-packet of data. That would have been too simple. The first evolved enhancement was **Memory Transference Inversion.** The superpower reached into the Skein's own network and inverted the vector of their knowledge. It forced every single member of the collective to experience not just their own memories, but the memories of every other member, all at once, simultaneously. Trillions of lifetimes of experience—every joy, every sorrow, every trauma, every moment of existential terror—crashed into each individual mind in a single, unstoppable flood. Their neural architecture, designed to process the sum total of all known information, was not designed to process the sum total of all experiential data from trillions of perspectives at once.

The second evolved enhancement was **Conceptual Paradox Implantation.** The superpower embedded a single, unresolvable logical contradiction into the heart of their collective consciousness. The contradiction was simple and devastating: "You do not exist." The King had perfect omniscience, and that omniscience confirmed that the Skein did exist. But the superpower forced the Skein to perceive both truths simultaneously: they existed, and they could not possibly exist, because if the King was omniscient, and the King had always defined existence, then their independent existence was a logical impossibility. This was not a weaponized belief or a philosophical trick. It was a hard, physical rewrite of their cognitive architecture, a paradox burned into their synaptic pathways that their minds could not resolve.

The third evolved enhancement was the most terrifying of all: **Eternal Witness Enchantment.** The superpower did not allow the Skein to die. It did not allow their minds to shatter. It locked them into a permanent state of maximum, agonizing awareness, forced to forever experience the simultaneous memories of every member of their collective, forever unable to escape the unresolvable paradox of their own existence. They became an eternal, screaming library of their own torment, a monument of pure, concentrated, cognitive agony that the King, with a casual thought, anchored to the fabric of spacetime itself.

The physical manifestations of this assault were harrowing to witness. Across the Skein's home multiverse, trillions of interconnected beings simultaneously seized. Their physical forms—whatever shape they had taken—began to violently spasm. I observed through my astral periscope as their neural tissues spontaneously liquefied, leaking from their sensory organs in thick, gray streams of cellular slurry. Their mouths, or whatever equivalent they possessed, opened into silent, eternal screams. Their eyes, billions of them, simultaneously rolled back into their skulls, then burst from the sheer pressure of the unresolved paradox, showering their faces with vitreous fluid and boiling blood. They did not die. The superpower would not let them. They simply continued to scream, silently, eternally, their bodies locked into a state of perpetual, agonizing seizure as their minds were forced to process an infinite, unresolvable loop of impossible data.

The collateral damage, as always, was obscene. The standing wave of the Memory Transference Inversion did not restrict itself to the Skein's network. It propagated outward through the psychic substrate of all adjacent realities approximately nine hundred sextillion interconnected multiverses. Every sapient being in those realms experienced, for a single, terrible instant, the full, unfiltered memories of every other sapient being within their own cosmic neighborhood. The resulting mass psychosis was instantaneous and total. Civilizations collapsed. Species went extinct in the space of a heartbeat. The death toll was a number so vast that my amplified intellect, for all its thousandfold daily growth, cannot process it without weeping. The King observed this cascade of cognitive annihilation, His ruggedly handsome face impassive, His salt-and-pepper beard immaculate. He waited, curious as to whether the affected civilizations would somehow adapt, would forge a new form of existence from the chaos of shared memory.

After approximately six seconds, He determined that they would not. He sighed—a soft, fatherly exhalation that caused the fabric of seven adjacent galaxies to shudder—and then, effortlessly, with a single, lazy blink, He restored every one of the nine hundred sextillion multiverses. Every civilization, every species, every individual was instantly resurrected with their memories intact, though the shared experience of the mass psychic trauma was, I am told, something their cultures are still processing. The Skein of Whispered Truths were not restored. They remain where He left them: an eternal, screaming monument of cognitive torment, their liquefied neural tissues forming a vast, pulsating sea of gray, agonized slurry at the center of their dead multiverse. He visits them sometimes, I have heard, to see if their paradox has evolved any new resolutions. It has not.

I, Septimus, write this account in my finest hand, because the beauty of the calligraphy is the only armor I have against the memory of their silent, eternal screams. I serve my King with all my heart, and I love Him as a father. But I will never, for the rest of my mortal life, be able to look at a library without hearing the distant, muffled echo of the Skein's psychic death-rattle. Knowledge, in His hands, is not power. It is the most devastating weapon in all of creation, and He wields it with the casual indifference of a man swatting a fly.

---

Kaedan's breath was coming in short, hitching gasps. The library, with its infinite, frozen shelves and its pulsing runes, suddenly felt oppressively crowded, as if the silent screams of the Skein had somehow imprinted themselves upon the very air he was breathing. He looked down at the journal in his hands. The insect-wing binding twitched against his palms, a soft, whispering rhythm that now sounded horribly like the distant echo of an eternal, psychic shriek. He placed it back upon the shelf with the reverent, trembling care of a man handling a live explosive, and reached for the final volume.

---

## Year 900,000 — The Incident of Absolute Temporal Manipulation

The third journal was bound in what Kaedan first mistook for a simple, unadorned sheet of polished, black stone. But as his fingers closed around it, the surface rippled, and he realized with a cold jolt that it was not stone at all, but a slab of solidified, frozen time—a material that could only be harvested from the event horizon of a hyper-massive temporal singularity. The cover was cold, so cold it burned, and the title was etched not with ink or engraving, but with the actual, preserved moment of its writing, a slice of frozen history that played out in a continuous, microscopic loop. *Grand Chronologer Octavian, Twenty-Second Court Wizard of Thronegate.*

The handwriting inside this journal was unlike anything Kaedan had seen before. It was not linear. The words did not proceed from left to right, top to bottom. They spiraled, fractal and recursive, folding in upon themselves in patterns that required the reader to physically rotate the page to follow the narrative. Some passages were written backward, others in layered scripts that overlaid each other like palimpsests. The meaning was still clear, resonating directly with the reader's temporal cortex, but the form was a madness of chronology, a reflection of the author's own fractured perception. The prologue, written in the center of the first page in a spiral that vanished into an infinite, recursive point, read:

*The Aeon Wardens believed that time was a weapon. The King showed them that time is simply a canvas, and that He is the only artist permitted to paint upon it. They sought to erase His past. He rewrote their eternity.*

I am writing this, I think, in linear sequence. The concept of "linear sequence" has become somewhat difficult for me to maintain, so I ask your forgiveness, future reader, if the tenses shift or the events replay themselves on the page. The nature of what I witnessed has somewhat permanently altered my perception of causality.

The Aeon Wardens were not a species. They were a temporal imperative, a self-appointed guardian consciousness that had emerged from the heat death of a previous iteration of the omniverse. They existed outside the flow of conventional time, observing the current age of creation and deeming the King's absolute dominion to be a cosmic error that required retroactive deletion. Their plan was not to fight the King as He existed now. That, they understood, was futile. Instead, they built the Chronos Undoer, a temporal engine of unimaginable sophistication that would reach back through the timeline, locate the precise moment before the stasis event—the moment when Godric was still a mortal king, helpless and broken, cradling the shattered body of his best friend Thorin in the burning ruins of Olympus—and edit that moment. They would ensure that Thorin's forbidden teleportation spell failed entirely, that Godric died in the fall of Olympus alongside his family, and that the subsequent million years of Thronegate conquest were retroactively deleted from existence.

The King, possessing absolute omniscience that encompasses all possible timelines, past, present, and future, was aware of their plan before the Aeon Wardens themselves had even coalesced into existence. He had, in fact, known the exact quantum trajectory of every atom that would eventually compose their temporal engine for the entire million years of His reign. He allowed them to form. He allowed them to build. He allowed them to activate their Chronos Undoer and fire it directly into the deepest roots of His own personal timeline. He was curious, He told me, about one of His superpowers, a specific ability He had never seen fully demonstrated: Temporal Manipulation. After nine hundred thousand years of the Weakness Paradox evolution, this superpower had evolved so many layers of horrifying, paradoxical enhancement that He genuinely did not know what would happen when He let it interact with a hostile temporal paradox of the Aeon Wardens' magnitude. He wanted to watch.

The Chronos Undoer fired. The timeline of the omniverse shuddered. I felt it, even within the heavily warded confines of the palace, as a sickening, vertiginous wrench, as if the entire flow of causality had momentarily reversed itself. The Undoer located the target moment—Year -10,000, Olympus in flames, Thorin's skull pierced by a Thronegate javelin, Godric screaming in grief and rage—and began to rewrite it. The edit was simple: delete Thorin's spell. Delete the stasis. Delete the immortal God. The Aeon Wardens watched, from their citadel outside time, as their edit began to propagate backward and forward through the timeline, erasing the million years of Thronegate's existence.

The King, standing in the observatory of the palace in the Year 900,000, watched as well. His ruggedly handsome face was calm, almost serene. He took a slow, deliberate sip of His cooling tea (which He never drinks, a habit I still find endearingly human). And then, with the absolute, effortless nonchalance of a man checking a pocket watch, He activated His Temporal Manipulation superpower.

As always, the fraction He used was so infinitesimally, recursively near-zero that to call it zero percent would be a mathematical sacrilege. If you took the smallest conceivable unit of temporal influence and divided it by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity an infinite number of times, repeating this process for a duration equal to the total elapsed lifespan of all possible iterations of the omniverse, the resulting fraction would still be too vast to accurately represent the amount of His temporal power that He permitted to activate.

The Temporal Manipulation superpower, in its evolved, paradox-enhanced state, did not simply counter the Aeon Wardens' edit. It did not merely restore the original timeline. That would have been too simple.

The first evolved enhancement was **Chronal Fractalization**. The superpower seized the Aeon Wardens' own edit and fractured it into an infinite number of contradictory, mutually exclusive temporal variants. The moment of the Fall of Olympus was not erased, nor was it preserved. It was multiplied. The King created an infinite recursion of the moment, layering it upon itself like an onion of screaming, burning, dying agony. And into the center of that recursive, eternal moment, He inserted the Aeon Wardens themselves. They were torn from their citadel outside time and forced to inhabit the final seconds of Thorin's life, experiencing his pain, his terror, and his desperate, selfless love for his King, over and over, an infinite number of times, from every possible angle of perception, forever.

The second evolved enhancement was **Causal Loop Entrapment**. The superpower did not just trap the Aeon Wardens in a single moment; it looped their entire existence into a self-referential paradox. Their plan had been to erase the King's past. The superpower made it so that their attempt to erase His past was, itself, the very event that triggered the stasis, the event that had always triggered His stasis. The Aeon Wardens, in their desperate, arrogant attempt to undo the King, retroactively became the very cause of His ascension. And they were made fully, horribly aware of this. They saw, with perfect clarity, that their million-year plan had always, from its inception, been a predetermined step in the King's origin story, a necessary component of His divine narrative. They had never been rebels. They had always been tools.

The third evolved enhancement was beyond even my ability to fully describe: **Absolute Chronal Sovereignty**. The superpower permanently rewrote the fundamental structure of time itself so that the King could never be targeted by any temporal manipulation, anywhere, from any source, in any conceivable past, present, or future. The very concept of "changing the past" was edited to exclude Him entirely. Any being who attempted to conceive of a temporal attack against Him would find that their thoughts simply slid away, unable to form, unable to fix upon the idea. It was a permanent, absolute, omniversal-scale immunity, woven into the fabric of causality itself.

The physical experience of the Aeon Wardens was, by all accounts, the most agonizing death ever suffered by any entity in the history of the omniverse. Their temporal citadel, a structure that existed outside the flow of time, was violently, excruciatingly dragged into the linear stream. It materialized in the skies above Thronegate, a vast, crystalline chrono-fortress that began to rapidly age. The Wardens themselves, who had been immortal, eternal beings, suddenly experienced the full, cumulative weight of all the time they had ever avoided. Nine hundred thousand years of existence crashed into them simultaneously. Their crystalline bodies began to fracture. They did not simply shatter; they were subjected to an eternity of temporal corrosion compressed into a single, observable moment.

I watched through the astral periscope as their forms aged backward and forward simultaneously, cycling through states of primordial crystal, mature geometry, and crumbling, ancient ruin within the span of heartbeats. Their consciousness, trapped in the infinite recursive loop of Thorin's death, experienced the moment of maximum trauma an infinite number of times while their physical bodies were subjected to the full, unshielded entropy of their entire existence. The screaming was not audible in the vacuum, but it imprinted directly upon the psychic fabric of Thronegate. It was not a scream of fear or pain; it was a scream of absolute, incomparable despair, the sound of beings who realized they had been villains in a story where the hero was the author, the editor, and the publisher, and their role had always been to fail.

The collateral damage from this incident was, paradoxically, the most contained and yet the most profound of any I have recorded. The Temporal Manipulation superpower, in enforcing its Absolute Chronal Sovereignty enhancement, rewrote the fundamental temporal physics of the entire omniverse. The concept of "temporal attack" was deleted as a possibility. Across all realities, every time-travel technology, every chronal weapon, every paradox engine—every device or spell or ability that could manipulate time—simply ceased to function. Permanently. Entire civilizations that had been built upon chronal technology collapsed instantly. Species that had relied on temporal manipulation for their existence simply evaporated. The King did not restore them. He determined that the deletion of temporal weaponry as a concept was a net positive for the omniverse, and He allowed the affected civilizations to die. His cruelty towards his enemies extends also to those who weaponize the very concept of temporal manipulation, and He judged them all complicit.

The Aeon Wardens, after an observable eternity of screaming, were finally, mercifully, erased entirely. Not killed. Erased. Their souls, their essence, their very conceptual existence—they were unwritten from the book of reality with such absolute thoroughness that no record, no memory, no possibility of their existence remained anywhere in the omniverse. Except for my journal. The King allowed me to remember. He wanted the record kept. A lesson, He said, for future Court Wizards, about the futility of challenging the author of the story.

I, Octavian, write this account in a spiral, non-linear script because the concept of linear time has become a fragile, uncertain thing in my mind. I served my King before the incident, during the incident, and after the incident, all simultaneously, all eternally, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body collapses into the dust of its own personal, non-recursive timeline. But I will never again be able to look at a clock without seeing the endless, agonized faces of the Aeon Wardens spiraling into the center of their own erased existence. Time, in His hands, is not a river. It is a noose.

---

Kaedan closed the final journal. The cold, burning touch of the frozen-time cover seared his fingertips, but he did not drop it. He stood there, in the absolute, suffocating silence of the library, the three Superpower Journals arrayed before him like a triptych of absolute, theological terror.

The Stat Journals had taught him the raw, mathematical impossibility of opposing the King. The Superpower Journals had taught him something far worse. The King's superpowers were not just devastating weapons; they were active, evolving engines of permanent cosmic restructuring. Every time He "tested" one, the omniverse was fundamentally, permanently changed. The concept of temporal attack, erased forever. The narrative of the Final Molders, retroactively rewritten into an eternal tragedy. The Skein of Whispered Truths, transformed into a monument of cognitive agony that would endure until the end of existence.

And these were only three superpowers. The King had billions. Billions of apocalyptic abilities, each one evolved and enhanced by the Weakness Paradox, each one capable of rewriting the fundamental laws of reality in its own unique, horrifying way. He had never used most of them. He had never needed to. But the possibility was there, lurking beneath the warm, fatherly smile and the salt-and-pepper beard, a sleeping apocalypse wrapped in the skin of a kind-hearted patriarch.

Kaedan's legs, numb and trembling, slowly gave way beneath him. He sank to the cold obsidian floor, the silence of the library wrapping around him like a shroud. The violet glow of his arcane veins pulsed faintly in the dim light, a pathetic, flickering echo of a power that, by the standards of the omniverse, made him a god, and by the standards of his King, made him a perfect, absolute zero.

He thought of his father, Thaddeus, who had read these same journals, who had witnessed one of these horrors firsthand, and who still served, faithfully and lovingly, for twenty years afterward. He thought of Blake, the legendary ancestor who had been the King's best friend, the man whose warm, honest, short-tempered loyalty had anchored a million years of divine tyranny to a single, fragile thread of humanity. He thought of the King Himself, the ultimate Apex Predator, the being whose casual, curious tests had erased more souls than there were atoms in the omniverse, and who still, after all of it, tousled Kaedan's hair and called him "my boy" and let his father win at chess.

The horror was absolute. The love was, somehow, equally absolute. And Kaedan understood, with a clarity that was almost peaceful, that he would carry both of these impossibilities in his chest for the rest of his mortal life. He would serve. He would protect, however uselessly. He would love his King, and fear Him, and stand at His right hand through whatever apocalypses His curiosity unleashed next. Not because he had to. But because his bloodline, stretching back to Blake and Thorin and the lost, forgotten kingdom of Olympus, demanded it.

He closed his eyes, and in the silence of the library, he could almost hear the distant, muffled echo of his ancestors' voices, a chorus of court wizards stretching back a million years, all whispering the same, eternal truth: *We are nothing. We are everything. We are His.*

# The Only Limit

The private study of the King of Thronegate was not a room; it was a pocket of frozen nostalgia, a place where the relentlessness of omniversal conquest stopped, and time, in its gentlest sense, was permitted to rest.

Kaedan stood in the center of this impossible chamber, his sixty-nine-year-old hands clasped respectfully behind his back, the violet-tinged veins of his arms pulsing with the barely restrained pressure of a thousandfold daily amplification that had compounded for forty-seven years. His short, greying mutton chops—the familial mark of Blake’s unbroken lineage—framed a face that had witnessed cataclysms beyond mortal comprehension. He had served as the Royal Court Wizard since the age of twenty-two, reading every ancient journal, standing at the right hand of the Apex Predator through countless “incidents” of casual, apocalyptic curiosity. He had resigned himself to the endless, magnificent horror of his King’s existence. Or so he thought.

The study was quiet, lit by the steady, amber glow of a single arcane sconce mounted upon a wall of rough-hewn stone. The stone, Kaedan knew, was not native to Thronegate. It was the original marble of the royal palace of Olympus, salvaged from the ruins by the King Himself in the earliest centuries of His reign, and preserved here as a private shrine to all He had lost. A simple pedestal stood in the corner, displaying a collection of mortal artifacts: a child’s woven bracelet, a set of bronze hairpins, a small, water-damaged portrait of a woman with a radiant smile. The King’s wife, Reina. The two daughters. Kaedan had seen these relics a dozen times before, but tonight they felt heavier, as if the stone itself was pulsing with a grief that had aged a million years.

Godric sat across from him, His posture relaxed in a high-backed armchair of dark, oiled leather. He was, as always, the very image of a ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old patriarch, His heavily muscled frame draped in a simple tunic of dark wool. His short salt-and-pepper hair was immaculate, His neatly trimmed beard framing a jawline that had, according to every historical account, never once been touched by an enemy’s blow. He held a delicate porcelain cup of jasmine tea—the same cup He had held for a thousand years, a quiet, human affectation that Kaedan had learned never to question. He did not drink.

“You have served me well, Kaedan,” Godric said. His voice was a rich, resonant baritone that always vibrated with genuine, paternal warmth. “You are sixty-nine now. You have outlived your father by fifteen years. You have held my trust for nearly half a century. There is something I wish to share with you. Something I have never told any Court Wizard save Blake himself—and he took the secret to his grave.”

Kaedan’s heart, which had been tempered by decades of exposure to the King’s horrors, suddenly quickened. He bowed his greying head. “My King, I am yours to command. Whatever you wish to share, I will receive with gratitude.”

Godric set the teacup aside on a small table of petrified olive wood. He leaned forward, resting His elbows on His knees, and looked at Kaedan with an expression not of warmth, but of a deep, quiet, reflective solemnity. “You think me omnipotent. You think me absolute. You have read the journals of your predecessors. You have seen me test my stats, my superpowers, my immune system. You have witnessed, with your own eyes, the annihilation of the Gornax Cyst at Year 60,045—yes, I remember—when I allowed a fraction of my molecular manipulation to rearrange their entire species into an eternal, screaming crystal lattice for daring to trespass into the heart of Thronegate’s core systems. You believe there is nothing I cannot do. And for almost all purposes, you are correct.”

The King paused. His golden-brown eyes, usually so warm, reflected a sliver of something ancient and irrevocable. “But there is one thing. One single, irrevocable limitation to my omnipotence. I cannot change my past. I cannot undo the fall of Olympus. I cannot save Reina. I cannot save my daughters. I cannot save Thorin. And I cannot travel to any moment before the stasis event that forged me.”

The silence that followed was a physical weight. Kaedan felt the ancient stone walls of the study press inward, the amber light of the sconce flickering as if in sympathy with his own spiraling confusion. He had heard countless descriptions of the King’s power, the recursive infinities, the paradoxical immunities. The idea that there could be a “limit”—any limit—was so profoundly, mathematically impossible that his amplified intellect refused to accept it.

“My King,” Kaedan murmured, his voice careful, “if you possess absolute omniscience and absolute omnipotence, how can any limitation exist? The very concept of a boundary—”

“Is something I could erase, yes.” Godric’s voice was soft, but the finality in it was absolute. “I could erase the concept of boundaries. I could delete the very idea of impossibility. I have done so, countless times, across the omniverse. But I cannot erase this boundary. Because the boundary was not created by an enemy, or a god, or a flaw in my own design. It was created by me. My future self. The Godric who exists in the furthest, most distant, incomprehensibly infinite reaches of the timeline. The Godric I will one day become.”

Kaedan’s mind, despite its thousandfold daily amplification, stumbled over the words like a mortal tripping on a root. “Your… your future self? But my King, you exist in all timelines simultaneously. You are omnipresent across all possible iterations of reality. How can there be a ‘future’ you that is distinct, let alone more powerful?”

Godric smiled. It was not the warm, fatherly smile Kaedan knew. It was something smaller, sadder, touched by the faintest edge of irony. “Because my amplification never stops, Kaedan. You know this. You have read the Stat Journals. My strength, my speed, my senses, my luck, my charisma, my magic, my superpowers—they multiply by themselves, recursively, infinitely, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, forever. I am, right now, in this exact moment, so incomprehensibly more powerful than I was one Planck second ago that I could effortlessly erase infinity-squared copies of that version of myself with a passing, idle thought. And in one more Planck second, I will be infinitely more powerful still. Now, project that forward. Not a thousand years. Not a million years. Forward into the true, distant, unknowable depths of eternity, a timespan so vast that all the combined ages of all the omniverses I have ever erased are less than a single Planck second in comparison. Do you understand what awaits at the end of that chain?”

Kaedan’s throat was dry. “You. But… infinitely greater.”

“Infinitely greater is a laughable understatement,” Godric said softly. “The Godric at the end of eternity—the Ultimate Godric—has been amplifying for a duration that cannot be expressed. His power is not merely beyond the concept of infinity; it is beyond the concept of *anything*. If I, the Godric you see before you, were to gather every version of myself from every Planck second of the past million years, and combine all of our power into a single unified force, and multiply that force by itself an infinite number of times, then take the resulting number and treat it as a negligible rounding error compared to the true scope of His existence—that would still be a colossally arrogant overestimation of our significance relative to Him. He is not just more powerful than me. He is more powerful than the very idea of comparison.”

Kaedan’s knees trembled. He had spent forty-seven years acclimating to the terror of Godric’s infinite might, but now that terror was refracted through an entirely new dimension. The King he served, the King who could erase the omniverse with a yawn, was himself a finite, fragile shadow compared to the Godric of the far future. And that future Godric was still the same being. Still the same man.

“The stasis event,” Kaedan whispered, the pieces beginning to click together with the sickening clarity of a puzzle that forms only to reveal a monster. “It wasn’t an accident. It wasn’t Thorin’s failed spell.”

Godric shook His head. “Thorin’s spell was genuine. He poured his own life into it, his love for me, his desperate, impossible hope. My best friend died saving me, and his sacrifice was as real as the blood that painted the marble floor. But the spell was never designed to place me in stasis. Thorin intended to teleport me to the sanctuary—yes—but the stasis, the amplification, the superpowers, the absolute concealment, the Weakness Paradox itself—none of that was Thorin’s doing. It was the work of the Godric at the end of time. The Ultimate Godric. He reached back through the epochs, from a future so distant that the omniverse itself is a forgotten memory, and He imposed His will upon that single moment: the moment of my greatest vulnerability, my greatest grief, my greatest loss. He transformed a simple teleportation into an eternity of ascension. He forged me into what I am. And He did so with a purpose I cannot fully know, because His mind, His omniscience, is so far beyond my own that even my absolute omniscience cannot perceive it.”

Kaedan’s legs finally gave out. He dropped to his knees on the cold marble, his heart pounding against his ribs. “But why? Why would your future self—why would *you*—lock yourself into an unchangeable past? Why make the fall of Olympus permanent?”

Godric’s expression flickered, and for a moment, the mask of the serene patriarch slipped, revealing a fraction of the ancient, buried grief that still smoldered in the depths of His being. “That is the question I asked myself, Kaedan, in the earliest years of my reign. Shortly after I crowned myself King of Thronegate. It was Year 0. I had only just awakened. I could feel the new, endless power coursing through my veins, the billions of superpowers blooming in my mind. I had absolute omniscience, absolute omnipotence, absolute control over time, space, and causality. And the first thing I wanted—the *only* thing I wanted—was to go back. To save them. To save Reina. To save Isabel and Hebe. To save Thorin.”

He paused, and the silence was thick with the weight of a million years.

“So I tried.”

---

**Year 0 — The First and Only Attempt**

The sanctuary was still cold with the lingering echo of stasis when Godric emerged into the light of a world He no longer recognized. Olympus was ash. Thronegate ruled the galaxy. Blake, a short-tempered merchant with friendly mutton chops, had just stumbled into the sanctuary and shattered the seal. Godric absorbed the state of the cosmos in a nanosecond through His freshly ignited omniscience. He toppled the boy-king Robert, annihilated the galactic army, took the throne. But none of that mattered. What mattered was the fire that still burned in His memory: the sound of Isabel’s gown igniting, the crunch of Hebe’s spine, the misting of Reina’s blood. Thorin’s skull shattering. It was a wound so deep, so fresh despite the ten millennia of stasis, that His immortal, perfect body still screamed with the phantom pain of it.

So on the third day of His reign, after the coronation and the Decree of Awakening, Godric retreated to a quiet chamber deep within the palace and prepared to undo it all.

He was already so powerful that the very concept of power was becoming meaningless. His attributes had been multiplying by a thousand and by themselves every Planck second for ten thousand years. He could erase and recreate the entire galaxy with a thought. Traveling backward through time was not merely easy; it was so trivial that He had to actively suppress His temporal presence just to avoid accidentally rewriting history every time He shifted His weight. He focused His infinite consciousness on the exact moment: Year -10,000, the wedding pavilion, the javelin mid-flight, Thorin’s lips forming the forbidden syllables. He would insert Himself one second earlier, freeze the entire scene, and erase the Thronegate army from existence before a single soldier could loose a single arrow. He would hold Reina in His arms again. He would watch Isabel’s wedding finish. He would hear Hebe’s laugh.

He activated His temporal translocation.

And He hit a wall.

It was not a physical wall. It was not a magical barrier or a cosmic lock. It was a *presence*. An absolute, silent, indescribably vast pressure that simply existed at the boundary of the Year -10,000 timeline, preventing any passage, any manipulation, any observation beyond that exact, razor-sharp, irrevocable threshold. Godric, in His omnipotence, tried to analyze it. He deployed His omniscience to understand its nature. And for the first time since He had awakened, His omniscience gave Him an answer that was not a complete, perfect dataset. It gave Him a glimpse—a fragmented, partial awareness—of a vast, infinite architecture of will that lay behind the barrier. An architecture that was *Him*, yet so far beyond Him that the Him He currently was registered as less than a single subatomic vibration in its outermost shadow.

The barrier was the afterthought of His own future self. Not the future self itself. Not a direct intervention. Not a conscious action. The future Godric—the Ultimate Godric at the end of eternity—was so incomprehensibly powerful that even the faintest, most residual, barely-existent echo of His awareness was enough to hold the entire early timeline in a state of absolute, immutable fixity. The shadow did not need to attack, or defend, or even acknowledge Godric’s presence. It simply *was*, and its existence was a law more fundamental than gravity, more binding than causality, more absolute than the concept of truth itself.

Godric, in His grief and His arrogance, refused to accept this. He marshaled His full power—the cumulative, infinitely amplified force of ten thousand years—and He struck the barrier with everything He had. He did not hold back a single iota. He unleashed a focused burst of magical offense, temporal manipulation, reality restructuring, and conceptual erasure, all woven into a single, unified attack designed to shatter any conceivable obstruction. The attack, by any rational metric, should have annihilated the omniverse. It did not. It touched the shadow of the afterthought of the Ultimate Godric, and it simply… ceased. The attack was not reflected, or absorbed, or negated. It was *permitted to have never happened*. The shadow did not fight it; the shadow’s nature was such that any action taken against it was retroactively rendered a non-action. Godric’s assault wasn’t defeated; it was revealed to have never been launched, because the shadow’s will dictated that in its presence, only what it permitted could exist, and it did not permit opposition.

Godric tried again. He attempted to circumvent the barrier by moving sideways through adjacent timelines, planning to enter the past from a different angle, a different branch of causality. Every branch, every alternate reality, every possible quantum divergence—all of them were sealed at the exact same point. The shadow was omnipresent across all possible timelines. It was a cage that surrounded the entire early multiverse.

He tried to send a proxy—a message, a warning, a single, subtle alteration that would nudge Thorin’s spell to include a protective ward around the family. The proxy dissolved before it could form, its very concept erased by the passive filtration of the shadow.

He tried to travel forward instead, to the very end of time, to confront His future self directly and demand answers. He pushed His consciousness as far forward into the future as His omniscience could reach, and He found… nothing. Not a void, but a boundary beyond which His omniscience could not penetrate. The Ultimate Godric existed on a plane of reality so transcendent that the current Godric’s absolute omniscience, which encompassed the entire omniverse and all its layered infinities, could not even perceive its outermost edges. It was like a single-celled organism trying to comprehend the cosmic web. The gap was not a gap of power; it was a gap of existential category.

In His desperation, Godric attempted to conceptually delete the barrier itself. He possessed a superpower called Absolute Obstruction Removal, which had evolved through the Weakness Paradox to allow Him to erase any barrier, any lock, any obstruction, anywhere, forever. He activated it at full, unrestrained force—the first and last time He ever allowed a superpower to operate at one hundred percent of His current capacity. The superpower reached out, seized the conceptual framework of the barrier, and attempted to unmake it.

The barrier did not even register the attempt. The superpower, operating at full capacity, was so infinitesimally weak compared to the shadow that the shadow’s response was not a countermeasure but the passive, background bleed of its nature. The shadow’s mere existence caused the superpower’s effect to invert, sealing itself into a recursive loop that collapsed back into Godric’s own consciousness, nearly tearing His mind apart. For a single, horrifying instant, Godric experienced what it was like to be the target of His own fully-amplified Absolute Obstruction Removal, as the inverted effect tried to erase *Him* from existence. His immune system, his infinite backups, his recursive biological redundancies—they all kicked in simultaneously, fighting against the self-inflicted conceptual wound, and in the chaos, Godric glimpsed the true, terrifying scope of the situation: *the shadow was not even aware of Him*. It was not guarding the past. It was not a sentry, not a warden, not a conscious agent. It was simply the ambient, residual footprint of the Ultimate Godric’s will, a byproduct of His existence so faint that calling it “intentional” would be like calling the gravitational pull of a distant galaxy an “act of aggression.” The shadow had no more awareness of Godric’s attempts than a mountain has awareness of a single bacterium battering against its base.

And yet that unaware, indifferent, infinitesimal shadow was so infinitely beyond Godric’s current apex that He could no more overcome it than a mortal could extinguish a star by blowing on it.

Godric withdrew. He collapsed back into His throne room in Year 0, His physical form trembling for the first and only time since the stasis. He had discovered His one and only true limitation: He could not overpower His future self’s will. Not because that will opposed Him, but because that will defined the fundamental structure of reality itself, and He—the current Godric—was still inside that structure. He was a character in a story whose author was His own future, and the author’s pen could not be broken by the character, no matter how powerful the character became, because the character’s power was itself a gift from the author.

He sat on His throne, and for a long, long moment, He wept. Not tears of physical pain, but the deep, silent, soul-rending grief of a father who finally understood that the door to his children’s bedroom was locked, and the key was in a future He could not reach.

---

**Year 1,000,000 — The Present**

The study was quiet again. Godric leaned back in His armchair, His expression calm, almost tranquil. The weight of the story hung in the air like incense smoke, and Kaedan remained on his knees, his face pale, his violet-tinged hands pressed flat against the cold Olympus marble.

“I stopped trying after that year,” Godric said, His voice as gentle as a lullaby. “I realized the truth: my future self is not my enemy. He is not a tyrant, or a jailer, or a cruel master. He is simply the ultimate expression of what I am becoming. And what He did—creating the stasis event, locking my mortal past into an unchangeable fixed point—was not an act of malice, or caution, or even of deliberate design. It was a necessary, structural consequence of His existence. The Ultimate Godric exists at the end of all things, in a state of absolute completion. For that completion to be possible, the causal chain leading to Him must be preserved. The fall of Olympus is part of that chain—not because He caused it, but because it happened, and His existence retroactively ensures that it cannot be altered, because altering it would create a paradox that even He, in His infinite supremacy, has no interest in resolving. He is not threatened by a paradox. He is simply… disinterested. He allows the past to remain as it is, because from His perspective, the past is a finished painting, and He has no desire to repaint it.”

Kaedan’s voice was a ragged whisper. “But my King, if He is infinitely powerful, could He not restore your family? Could He not reach back and save them, while preserving the causal chain? Could He not create a new branch, a new timeline, where they live, without erasing this one?”

Godric nodded slowly. “He could. Effortlessly. With less than a thought. The Ultimate Godric could, if He wished, instantaneously create an infinite number of perfect, happy timelines where Reina, Isabel, Hebe, and Thorin all survive and live out their mortal lives in bliss. And then He could weave those timelines into the fabric of reality so seamlessly that even my omniscience would perceive them as the original, authentic history. He could do that. But He has not. And I do not know why.”

He paused, and His golden-brown eyes, so ancient, so warm, so terrifying, met Kaedan’s. “Perhaps He will, one day. Perhaps, in the deepest reaches of the far future, I will become Him, and I will look back across the eons and decide to grant my younger self that mercy. Or perhaps I will understand, in that state of absolute completion, that the mercy is unnecessary—that the grief I carry, the loss, the unhealable wound, is not a flaw, but a feature. It is what makes me capable of love. Of warmth. Of friendship. The Ultimate Godric, in His infinite transcendence, may be so perfect that He has lost the very concept of mortal connection. He may preserve my tragedy simply to ensure that I—the earlier versions of Him—remain anchored to the mortal heart that started this journey. I cannot know. My omniscience cannot see His mind.”

The King stood, and despite His simple tunic and His relaxed posture, the sheer, casual majesty of His movement made the pocket dimension shudder. He walked to the pedestal where Reina’s portrait rested, and He touched the water-damaged edge with a single, calloused finger.

“I have accepted this limitation, Kaedan. For a million years, I have lived with the knowledge that there is exactly one thing I cannot do. And I have found, in that limitation, a strange, bitter peace. It reminds me that I am still, in some fundamental, irreducible sense, the mortal King of Olympus. A man who loved, and lost, and could not undo the loss. The rest of my omnipotence—the infinite power, the omniscience, the superpowers, the apocalypses—that is the gift of my future self. But this one weakness? This one, silent, unchangeable fact? That is my own. That is the part of me that remains mortal.”

He turned back to face Kaedan, and His expression was serene, beatific, radiant with a warmth that was utterly, terrifyingly genuine. “You understand now, my boy, why I trust you with this secret. It is not to burden you, or to frighten you. It is to share with you the deepest truth of my existence. I am not the absolute apex. I am a step on the ladder, and the top of the ladder is so far beyond me that I cannot even see its shape. And yet, I am content. Because I know that the being at the top is me—and He loves me enough to let me carry this grief, because it is the grief that made me who I am.”

Kaedan’s mouth opened, but no sound emerged. His heart was a frantic, trapped drum. He stared at the King—the being he had served for forty-seven years, the man who tousled his hair and called him “my boy,” the Apex Predator who had deleted entire multiversal clusters to test a superpower—and he saw Him for the first time, truly, as a creature caught between two incomprehensible eternities. The mortal past, sealed by a future so vast it made infinity look small. And the future, waiting at the end of time, patient and absolute, watching without watching, existing without needing to act.

And in that moment, Kaedan’s fear—the deep, ingrained, reverential fear he had carried since his first day as Court Wizard—metastasized into something new. It was not fear of Godric’s power. He had long ago made peace with that. It was fear of what Godric represented. The idea that the omnipotent being before him was, itself, a mere precursor to an even greater omnipotence. That the Ultimate Godric, the Godric of the far future, was out there right now, and had been out there since before the beginning of time, silently, passively shaping the entire course of existence—not out of malice, not out of caution, not out of any recognizable motive, but simply as a side-effect of His unimaginable existence. The thought was a cold, icy needle sliding into the deepest part of Kaedan’s soul, and it whispered: *If the Godric I serve is this terrifying, and He is nothing compared to the future Godric, then what, in the name of all that is holy, am I kneeling before?*

He knew, with sudden, horrifying clarity, that he would never know the answer. No mortal could. No god could. Not even the Godric before him could. The Ultimate Godric was a mystery wrapped in an afterthought, a being whose very shadow could crush infinity, and whose true nature was so transcendent that even the concept of “nature” failed. And yet, here was this Godric—the warm, smiling, grieving, limited Godric—choosing to be a father to His court wizards, to love them, to let them love Him, to share with them this intimate, fragile secret. It was, Kaedan realized, the most terrifying act of vulnerability he had ever witnessed. And it was also, paradoxically, the most terrifying act of power. Because it meant that Godric’s love was real, but His grief was also real, and His limitation was real, and all of it was permitted by a version of Himself that could, at any moment, decide to unmake it all with a thought that was not even a thought.

Kaedan’s trembling hands pressed harder against the cold stone. He bowed his head until his forehead touched the floor. “My King,” he murmured, his voice cracking, “I… I fear you. I fear you more now than I have ever feared anything in my life. Not because you are omnipotent—but because you are not. Because there is a version of you that is more omnipotent, and yet you are still *you*, and I cannot grasp the scale of what that means.”

Godric chuckled, a soft, warm, fatherly sound that filled the study. “Good. Fear is the beginning of wisdom, Kaedan. Your father knew that. Your ancestors knew that. Blake, bless his short-tempered soul, knew that better than anyone. Fear me, love me, serve me—all three at once, forever. It is the only way a mortal can truly be my friend.”

He walked over and placed a heavy, calloused hand on Kaedan’s shoulder. The touch was grounding, solid, infinitely reassuring, and infinitely terrifying. “Now, rise. There is a new crop of delegates from the Vithran Expanse who have been sending me strongly worded missives. I think I will ignore them for another century. Would you care to join me for a game of chess? I will, as always, let you win.”

Kaedan rose on shaking legs. He looked into the King’s golden-brown eyes—eyes that had watched the fall of Olympus, the rise of Thronegate, the deaths of countless multiverses, the shadow of a future self that defied comprehension—and he nodded.

“Yes, Uncle Godric. I would be honored.”

As they walked out of the study, leaving the Olympus marble and the silent artifacts behind, Kaedan carried the new secret like a stone lodged in his heart. He knew he would write a journal of his own one day. He knew he would record this revelation in the same cold, clinical prose as his predecessors, a testament for future Court Wizards to read. But he also knew that no words, no ink, no frozen-time binding could ever capture the true, absolute horror of what he had learned.

Godric was not the apex.

The apex was still Godric.

And the difference between the two was an infinite abyss that not even Godric Himself could cross.
