# The Playful God

The throne room of Thronegate was silent save for the low, rhythmic hum of the perpetually orbiting chrono-spheres that circled the obsidian dais like nervous fireflies. Godric lounged upon His throne, His heavily muscled frame draped in what appeared to be a comfortable, slightly faded tunic of simple grey linen. His salt-and-pepper hair was, as always, immaculate, and His neatly trimmed beard framed the rugged, handsome face of a man who looked no older than forty-five. He had been staring at the ceiling for the past three years, not out of melancholy or introspection, but simply because He found the shifting patterns of the frozen star-matter embedded in the vaulted obsidian to be mildly more entertaining than the endless, sycophantic reports from the newly conquered Xeranthian Expanse.

Beside Him, standing at rigid attention, was Theron, His current Court Wizard. The boy—for he was a boy of twenty-two, the latest in the unbroken line of Blake—was still fresh in his office, his formal robes stiff with new embroidery, his face a pale mask of carefully controlled reverence. Godric liked him. He had the same blunt, short-tempered expression as his ancestor, though he was too terrified to express it yet.

“Theron,” Godric said, His voice a deep, resonant baritone that vibrated pleasantly through the obsidian. “Do you know what the most amusing thing is about absolute omniscience?”

The young wizard flinched at the sudden address, then quickly shook his head. “No, my King. I… I cannot pretend to understand the scope of Your awareness.”

Godric chuckled, a warm, fatherly sound that seemed entirely at odds with the cold dread that permanently lingered in the throne room. “It’s that nothing surprises you. Every possible outcome of every possible action, stretching forward an infinite number of Planck seconds—I have already perceived it all. I have watched the birth and death of every universe, every civilization, every single atom, a decillion times over. I have seen every possible version of your life, your death, your choices. It is, in its own terrible way, profoundly boring.” He turned His gaze from the ceiling to meet Theron’s wide eyes. “So, to keep myself entertained, I sometimes choose to… ignore. I pick a reality, a universe, a single mote of dust in the endless grinding of the omniversal gears, and I deliberately do not look at its future. I give myself the gift of suspense. Do you understand?”

Theron’s throat bobbed. “You blind yourself, my King? But Your omniscience—”

“Is still active. I merely choose to restrain myself from extrapolating forward. I can still see every atom of that universe in the present moment, but I do not let myself see the next Planck second until it arrives. It is a form of meditation. And today, I feel like exercising that little gift. There is a universe I have not touched since before the Fall of Olympus. A universe so primitive, so isolated, so blissfully unaware of the larger omniverse that its inhabitants have invented their own gods, their own myths, their own quaint little philosophies. They call their home ‘Earth.’” Godric’s lips curled into a smile that did not quite reach His golden-brown eyes. “I think it’s time I introduced Myself.”

Theron, to his credit, did not immediately vomit. He had read the Stat Journals, the Superpower Codices, and the grim memoirs of a thousand Court Wizards before him. He knew exactly what happened when the King expressed an interest in a new universe. “My King,” he said carefully, “shall I prepare the formal terms of subjugation? The standard offer of unconditional surrender, with a thirty-minute grace period before the—”

“No, no,” Godric waved a hand dismissively. “No formal terms. No grace period. I’m feeling… mischievous today.” He rose from the throne with the fluid, silent grace of a predator, His bare feet echoing softly on the cool obsidian. “This will be a game. They will try to kill Me, of course—they always do, these proud little mortals. And I will let them. I will give them every opportunity, every desperate strategy they can conceive. And then, one by one, in the most creative and excruciating manners I can devise, I will kill them all. Not a quick death. Not a clean erasure. I want to hear them scream, Theron. I want their agony to echo across their local dimension for a million years. I want the universe to remember their suffering as a fundamental law of its own physics.”

Theron’s face had gone the color of old milk. He swallowed hard. “Is there… a reason this universe has earned Your particular displeasure, my King?”

Godric paused, His head tilting slightly as if considering the question. “No. None at all. That’s what makes it fun.”

He took a single step forward, and the fabric of space folded around Him like a cloak. The throne room vanished, replaced in a flicker by the cold, endless void of a universe that had never known the touch of a god.

---

Godric materialized in the upper thermosphere of Earth, His body suspended effortlessly among the wispy trails of dying satellites and the faint, blue curvature of the planet below. He looked down at the spinning marble of continents, the swirling white storms, the glittering lights of cities turning on as night crept across the hemisphere. He allowed His omniscience to wash over the planet in a single, gentle wave, absorbing every detail of its civilization—their languages, their wars, their art, their desperate little dreams, and above all, their fiction. Oh, how rich their fiction was! They had written stories about gods, about demons, about all-powerful entities from beyond the stars. They had imagined cosmic horrors, alien invasions, and even a few scattered tales that came eerily close to the truth. Godric read every book, every screenplay, every half-formed idea in the mind of a daydreaming child, and He felt a genuine, warm amusement.

“They’ve been waiting for Me,” He murmured to Himself, a soft chuckle vibrating through the empty void. “They just don’t know it.”

He descended. Not as a meteor, or a beam of light, or a majestic sky-chariot, but as a man. He simply allowed gravity to reassert its gentle pull, and He drifted downward like a falling leaf, His simple grey tunic fluttering in the thin upper winds. He passed through the stratosphere, the troposphere, until He could smell the faint pollutants, the salt of the ocean, the exhaust of a billion combustion engines. He landed softly, His bare feet touching the cracked asphalt of a minor road in a small town in the American Midwest. The sun was setting, painting the sky in shades of orange and purple, and a few cars hummed lazily along the distant highway.

Godric looked around at the humble buildings, the grain silos, the faded billboards advertising cheap beer and car insurance. He took a deep breath, savoring the air—it was polluted, yes, but it was real, unfiltered by arcane wards or dimensional shielding. And then, with the gentle, paternal smile of a father about to play a game with his children, He released His absolute concealment—not fully, never fully, but just enough for them to hear His voice.

Every human on Earth simultaneously froze. The voice did not come from the sky or from radios or from the earth. It simply manifested inside their minds, bypassing the ears entirely, in whatever language they spoke most intimately. It was a warm, resonant baritone, gentle and polite, like a neighbor asking to borrow a cup of sugar.

“Citizens of Earth,” Godric said. “My name is Godric, King of Thronegate, Apex Predator of the Omniverse. I am here to formally annex your world into my dominion. In one hour, I will begin the process of subjugation. You are, of course, welcome to resist. I encourage it. Please, use every weapon, every strategy, every clever little trick your species has devised over its brief existence. I will be waiting in the town of Oakhaven, Nebraska.”

The voice faded, leaving behind a silence that was immediately, violently shattered by global panic.

---

The hour was a spectacle that Godric watched with genuine, childlike curiosity. His omniscience, though deliberately restrained from forward-extrapolation, allowed Him to witness every corner of the planet simultaneously. He saw the United Nations Security Council convene in emergency session, their faces pale and sweating as they debated whether the voice was a global hallucination or a genuine extraterrestrial contact. He saw the Pentagon scrambling to locate Him, satellites swiveling their lenses toward the coordinates that had been psychically imprinted on every human mind. He saw religious leaders falling to their knees, some proclaiming the Apocalypse, others declaring Him the Antichrist, still others weeping in ecstatic rapture because their gods had finally spoken.

And He saw the soldiers. The first ones arrived within minutes, a convoy of armored vehicles roaring down the empty highway toward Oakhaven. The town itself had already been evacuated; Godric had psychically nudged the residents to leave, not out of mercy, but because He wanted a clear stage. The soldiers fanned out, their assault rifles trained on Him as He stood in the middle of the town square, His hands clasped behind His back, His expression placidly curious.

“Unknown target!” a voice boomed over a loudspeaker. “Kneel with your hands on your head immediately!”

Godric tilted His head, amused. He did not kneel. Instead, He lifted one hand and gave a small, friendly wave. “Hello. I’m not going to do that. But I’d like to see your weapons. Please, fire at will.”

The order came a moment later, and the air was filled with the deafening roar of gunfire. Bullets—hundreds of them, of varying calibers—streaked toward Godric’s body. He did not flinch. He did not raise a shield. He simply let them hit. The kinetic force of the rounds, minuscule compared to the infinite mountain of His strength, transferred to His skin with less effect than a gentle summer breeze. The bullets didn’t bounce; they simply evaporated on contact, their mass converted to harmless photons in a faint puff of violet light. The soldiers, watching their finest small-arms fire achieve less than nothing, began to scream in a mixture of terror and disbelief.

“That was excellent,” Godric said warmly. “Now, please escalate. I know you have nuclear weapons. I’ll wait.”

The next twenty minutes were a crescendo of human desperation. Godric allowed them to throw everything they had at Him, not because He needed to test anything—He already knew the precise outcome of every weapon they could ever conceive—but because He was savoring the slow, dawning horror in their collective psyche. They launched cruise missiles, then hypersonic glide vehicles, then experimental directed-energy cannons. Each attack struck with enough force to level a small city, and each dissolved into harmless light upon touching His skin. Godric stood in the center of the cratered, burning town square, His grey tunic unstained, His salt-and-pepper hair barely rustled.

The nuclear strike came from a submarine, a single MIRV warhead that split into six reentry vehicles, each descending toward Oakhaven with the destructive power of several megatons. Godric watched the bright, blinding flashes with a nostalgic fondness, reminded of a supernova He had once casually consumed for its mild, peppery flavor. The nuclear fireball engulfed Him, the shockwave flattening everything for miles around. And still, He stood. The mushroom cloud rose, and He was still there, arms now crossed over His broad chest, His expression mildly disappointed.

“That’s it?” He called out, His voice effortlessly carrying across the smoke and radiation to the distant drone cameras that were relaying the footage. “That’s the best you have? Your scientists have been dreaming of antimatter weapons, quantum disintegration, biological agents. I’m right here. Try them. I can wait.”

They tried. Over the remaining minutes, a frantic, desperate race of scientific madness unfolded. Labs around the world were commanded to deploy every experimental weapon. Godric allowed a few of them to actually strike Him—a prototype antimatter bomb detonated in high orbit, a cloud of weaponized smallpox, even a crudely aimed laser designed to induce a false vacuum decay in the local quantum field. He absorbed them all with the same casual indifference, and slowly, He allowed the reality of their futility to sink into every human mind. Not through psychic manipulation, but simply by letting them watch the footage, letting them analyze the data, letting them understand that nothing worked.

The hour ended.

“Time’s up,” Godric said, and the entire world heard Him.

Then the game truly began.

---

Godric had not lied when He said He wanted their deaths to be agonizing. He began with the soldiers who had fired on Him—the ones who were still alive, huddled in the ruins of their armored vehicles, their minds broken. He lifted a single finger, and every soldier in a fifty-mile radius simultaneously felt their nervous systems become hyper-aware. Every nerve, every pain receptor, was amplified a billionfold. The gentle pressure of their own uniforms against their skin became unbearable torment. The air itself, moving against their exposed flesh, felt like a constant, grinding sandstorm of razor blades. They screamed. They clawed at their own bodies, trying desperately to escape the sensations that had become pure, unending agony. And Godric watched, His expression placid, as they slowly killed themselves in their thrashing—ripping off their own clothes, then their own skin, then their own muscles, driven to a frenzy of self-destruction just to make the pain stop. It took hours, because He would not let them die quickly. Their hearts kept beating, their lungs kept gasping, long after their bodies should have failed.

And that was only the beginning.

He expanded His attention to the United States military command structure. Every general, every admiral, every politician who had authorized the attack—He reached into their minds and extracted their deepest, most personal fears. For one, it was the fear of being buried alive. Godric obliged, opening a chasm beneath the man’s feet that swallowed him into a pocket dimension of absolute, suffocating darkness, where he would remain conscious and gasping for air for the next ten thousand years, compressed seconds into eons. For another, it was the fear of his children dying. Godric appeared beside the man in his bunker, took the form of his youngest daughter, and then, while the man watched, He slowly, methodically peeled the image’s skin off, layer by layer, each scream precisely calibrated to be indistinguishable from the real daughter’s voice. When the man’s heart finally gave out from sheer terror, Godric restarted it and did it again, three times, before letting him die permanently.

Then He turned to the wider population.

Godric was not indiscriminate. He did not simply snap the planet out of existence, though it would have been laughably easy. He wanted each death to be a unique, personalized masterpiece of suffering. He began with the leaders of the nations who had refused to surrender—picking them up, one by one, with invisible hands, and forcing them to experience the sum total of all the pain they had ever inflicted on others, amplified and condensed into a single, eternal moment. But He quickly grew bored of the leaders. They were too few. So He expanded His reach to the cities—the great metropolises of Earth, the teeming billions who, in their collective ignorance, had not yet fully grasped what was happening.

He chose Tokyo first. In an instant, He psychically linked every human in the greater metropolitan area into a single, shared sensory mesh. And then He sent a pulse of what He called, in His own mind, a “suffering cascade.” One person’s stubbed toe became everyone’s stubbed toe, recursively amplified through the mesh until the sensation was a continuous, shrieking wave of phantom agony. People collapsed in the streets, clutching their feet—and then their hands, their heads, their hearts—as the cascade spread, each new pain adding to the collective scream. The mesh fed on itself, and Godric, gentle as a composer conducting a symphony, adjusted the frequencies to ensure that the agony never killed them too quickly. He let it play out over twelve hours, the entire city an orchestra of writhing, dying, still-conscious bodies. By the time the last heart stopped, Tokyo was a silent, glistening field of broken flesh, steam rising from the tangled masses as the residual neural energies dissipated.

He repeated the performance in Delhi, in New York, in London, in Shanghai—each with a different, creative twist. In Delhi, He altered their perception of temperature so that the very air felt like molten lead pouring into their lungs, while their bodies remained physically unharmed. In New York, He made every person see their own reflection as a rotting, maggot-infested corpse, driving them to suicide by hurling themselves through windows or into traffic, only to find that He wouldn't let them die—they lay, shattered and broken on the streets, conscious and in agony, until He finally released them. In London, He twisted their sense of time, so that each second lasted a subjective year, and during that year they experienced every grief, every regret, every moment of existential despair they had ever suppressed, until their minds simply collapsed.

The global military, in its final, desperate hours, turned to more exotic solutions. A secret cabal of occultists, who had long dabbled in the fringe rituals of a forgotten era, attempted to summon a "greater demon" to oppose Godric. He found this so delightful that He allowed their summoning to actually work—except that He possessed the summoned entity, wore it like a meat puppet, and used its claws to personally disembowel the cultists while laughing in their own language. Another group of physicists attempted to create a kugelblitz, a black hole made of pure radiation, hoping to swallow Him. Godric helpfully let them complete the device, then swallowed the black hole Himself and burped out a stream of gamma rays that cooked the physicists where they stood, their bodies flash-frying in an instant but their consciousnesses preserved, screaming inside their own skulls, for a subjective eternity.

He saved the children for last—not out of any mercy, but because their fear was the purest, the most exquisitely unfiltered. Godric appeared to them in the forms of their favorite cartoon characters, their beloved superheroes, and while they stared in bewildered joy, He transformed those forms into shambling, rotting horrors that devoured their parents in front of them, then turned to the children themselves. The terror in their eyes, He noted, was a rare vintage, a flavor He had not savored in millennia.

Throughout the entire ordeal, which stretched across a subjective week of Earth-time but only a few hours of Godric's relaxed, leisurely pace, He maintained the same warm, friendly demeanor. When survivors begged Him for mercy, He smiled gently and explained that He was giving them exactly what they had asked for: a chance to kill Him. They had tried. They had failed. Now they were paying the price. It was, He said, only fair.

By the end, the surface of Earth was unrecognizable. The oceans had boiled from the residual energies of His various torments, though He had kept the planet physically intact to prolong the suffering of those who fled to underground bunkers. The atmosphere was a churning soup of ash, radiation, and the lingering psychic screams of ten billion humans, each preserved as a frozen echo in the quantum substrate of the planet. Godric, standing on the peak of what had once been Mount Everest, surveyed His handiwork with a quiet, paternal pride.

He did not restore them. He did not feel the slightest flicker of remorse. These ants had been offered the chance to submit peacefully, and they had chosen defiance. The price of defiance was, and always would be, absolute.

He turned His attention to the rest of the universe—the handful of fledgling civilizations on a few distant exoplanets, the microbial life drifting in the void, the potential for future intelligence. He considered, for a moment, whether to let them live. They had not personally opposed Him, after all. But they were part of the same universe, the same seed of arrogance. He decided to be thorough.

With a single, infinitesimal flex of His molecular manipulation superpower—using a fraction so small that calling it zero percent was a grandiose overestimation—Godric issued a single command to every atom in the universe: *forget your cohesion.* The fabric of matter across the entire cosmos, from the burning hearts of stars to the cold dust of distant nebulae, instantly dissolved into a fine, luminous mist. Every solid, liquid, gas, and plasma, every complex molecule, every life-form on every world, was simultaneously and irrevocably unraveled. The universe went dark, all energy dissipating into a uniform, featureless void.

Godric stood alone in that void, the only entity in existence, surrounded by the faint, fading heat of a cosmos that had just been unceremoniously deleted. He took one last, nostalgic look at the spot where Earth had been—where the screams still echoed in the depleted quantum foam—and He smiled.

“That was fun,” He murmured to Himself. Then He folded space and stepped back into the throne room.

Theron was still standing exactly where he had been hours earlier, his face pale as death, his hands trembling. The young wizard had, Godric knew, been watching the entire event through an astral projection the King had kindly left open for him. A learning experience, He’d called it.

“Well, my boy,” Godric said, settling back into His throne with a satisfied sigh. “That was an invigorating diversion. They had such creative imaginations. I’ll have to visit a few more untouched universes soon. Remind me, won’t you?”

“Y-yes, my King,” Theron whispered, his voice barely audible.

Godric beamed at him, the warm, fatherly smile returning in full force. “Don’t look so glum. Come, have some tea. I’ve brought back a souvenir.” He produced from thin air a small, charred, yet perfectly intact smartphone, still displaying a frozen, terrified face on its cracked screen. “They called this a ‘selfie.’ I think it captures the moment rather well, don’t you?”

Theron fainted. Godric chuckled, poured Himself a cup of jasmine tea, and settled in for a pleasant evening of reflection. The Apex Predator was, for the moment, content.

# The Final Omniversal War

**Year 1,444,250 — From the Chronicle of Rutger, Eighty-Ninth Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The observatory of the imperial palace was a sphere of perfect, crystalline silence, suspended in the void between the surviving layers of ordered reality. I, Rutger, stood at the right hand of the eternal throne, my ceremonial robes clinging to my shoulders with the cold sweat of anticipation. I am forty-one years old, a direct descendant of Blake's unbroken lineage, and my arcane power has been multiplying by a thousandfold every single day since my twentieth birthday. By the standards of any mortal civilization, I am a walking cataclysm. By the standards of my King, I am less than a rounding error in the mathematics of infinity.

The reason for my presence was simple: the unthinkable had finally happened.

Across all fictional universes—every narrative reality, every imagined cosmos, every story that had ever been told or would ever be conceived—a united force had assembled. Not a coalition of necessity or a desperate alliance of remnants. This was a total, absolute unification. Every character, every god, every abstract entity, every cosmic principle, every protagonist and antagonist from every fictional framework that had ever existed had set aside their eternal conflicts and united under a single, impossible banner: the death of Godric.

The King sat upon His throne, His posture relaxed, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the cold, distant light of the mustering armada. He was, as always, the image of a forty-five-year-old patriarch, His short salt-and-pepper hair immaculate, His neatly trimmed beard framing a jawline that had never once known the touch of an enemy's strike. He held a delicate porcelain cup of jasmine tea—the same cup He had held for over a million years—and He did not drink. His golden-brown eyes, warm and crinkled with genuine amusement, surveyed the gathering host.

"Rutger," Godric said, His voice a rich, resonant baritone that vibrated comfortably in my sternum, "do you know what I find most delightful about this moment?"

I swallowed, my throat dry. "No, my King."

"The sheer, breathtaking hubris," He said, a soft chuckle escaping His lips. "They have united. Every faction. Every god. Every hero and every villain. They have set aside their petty squabbles—the eternal wars between heaven and hell, the cosmic rivalries, the philosophical schisms—all of it, erased in a single, desperate bid to destroy me. And do you know what that means, Rutger?"

I shook my head mutely.

"It means," Godric said, His smile widening into something sharp and predatory, "that they have finally given Me a reason to stop holding back. Not My power—I will hold back, as always, to an infinitesimal, near-zero fraction. But My creativity. My imagination. I have been so terribly bored, Rutger. So terribly, terribly bored." He set the teacup aside with a soft, porcelain click. "Let us see what the united fictions of all creation can offer. Let us give them their grand, glorious war. And then, let us show them why stories are meant to end."

---

**The Mustering of the Fictional Omniverse**

I observed through the astral scrying matrix as the armada assembled in the empty spaces between multiverses. The scale was, I must admit, genuinely impressive. Not threatening—nothing could threaten the King—but impressive in the way a particularly ambitious anthill might impress a boot.

The vanguard was composed of the cosmic hierarchies. From the Cthulhu Mythos, the Outer Gods had emerged in their full, maddening glory: Azathoth, the Blind Idiot God, whose mere existence was a nuclear chaos at the center of all infinity, surrounded by a seething court of pipers and dancers. Yog-Sothoth, the All-in-One and One-in-All, a congeries of iridescent spheres that contained every point in space and time simultaneously. Nyarlathotep, the Crawling Chaos, whose thousand forms flickered and shifted as He moved, His endless hunger for suffering and madness now focused into a single, razor-sharp purpose. Shub-Niggurath, the Black Goat of the Woods with a Thousand Young, her vast, writhing bulk pulsing with the endless fecundity of her monstrous offspring.

Beside them, the cosmic entities of the Marvel and DC multiverses had set aside their eternal struggles. The One Above All, a being of pure, radiant light whose presence was said to be the absolute pinnacle of that fictional framework, floated in serene, terrible majesty. The Presence, his counterpart from the DC cosmos, manifested as a vast, booming voice that echoed through the void. The Living Tribunal, whose three faces represented equity, necessity, and vengeance, hovered in judgment. The Spectre, the embodiment of divine wrath, burned with green, holy fire. The Endless—Dream, Death, Destiny, Desire, Despair, Delirium, and Destruction—stood in a silent, solemn row, their aeonic powers woven into a single, unified cord.

And then there were the warriors. The ones who would actually attempt to strike the blow.

From the world of anime and manga, a legion of reality-shattering combatants had assembled. Son Goku, the Saiyan warrior whose power had scaled beyond the concept of scaling itself, stood at the forefront, his silver hair blazing with the perfected Ultra Instinct, his body humming with an energy that could erase entire multiverses with a casual punch. Beside him, the Grand Zeno, the Omni-King, whose childlike face belied an ability to delete entire timelines from existence with a single thought, floated in his characteristic, detached silence. The angels of the Dragon Ball cosmos—Whis, Vados, the Grand Priest—formed a phalanx of supreme martial and temporal mastery, their staffs glowing with the accumulated power of a trillion years of divine training.

From a darker corner of the fictional multiverse, the Anti-Spiral had emerged, its vast, biomechanical form pulsating with the accumulated despair of a species that had sacrificed its own evolution to become the ultimate weapon of absolute, nihilistic survival. Beside it, the Ideon, a vast, god-machine whose very activation had once triggered the death of an entire universe, hummed with apocalyptic potential. Getter Emperor, the evolving, self-perpetuating god of will and evolution, its form cycling through infinite permutations of ever-increasing power. The STTGL—Super Tengen Toppa Gurren Lagann—a mecha so vast it dwarfed superclusters, piloted by the indomitable spirit of Simon the Digger, whose spiral energy was theoretically infinite and unstoppable.

From the realms of dark fantasy and grimdark, a different kind of power had gathered. The God-Emperor of Mankind, His shattered, skeletal form strapped to the Golden Throne, had been temporarily restored to His full, pre-Heresy glory by a coalition of the most powerful psykers from across the fictional spectrum. His golden armor burned with the fire of a billion souls, His psychic presence so overwhelming that the fabric of reality itself screamed in His wake. The Chaos Gods—Khorne, Tzeentch, Nurgle, and Slaanesh—had, through an impossible, temporary pact, fused their conflicting essences into a single, undivided entity of pure, primordial ruination. The Aeldari gods, the C'Tan, the Old Ones—all the ancient, terrible powers of the Warhammer multiverse had assembled, their aeons of war suspended for this single, impossible moment.

And from the highest tiers of conceptual fiction, the truly abstract beings had deigned to participate. Featherine Augustus Aurora, the Witch of Theatergoing, whose power was literally to rewrite the script of existence as she saw fit, floated in a throne of woven narrative threads. The Creator from Umineko, the supreme being of that fictional framework. The Chousin—Tokimi, Tsunami, and Washu—the goddesses who had created the entire Tenchi Muyo multiverse as an experiment, and who existed beyond even the highest dimensions of reality. The Lord of Nightmares from Slayers, the primordial chaos that was the source of all things. Akasha, the root of all existence from the Nasuverse. Each one of them was a being that, in their own native framework, was considered absolutely omnipotent and absolutely invincible.

And behind them all, filling the void with an endless, glittering sea of power, were the countless billions of lesser warriors, mages, heroes, villains, monsters, and gods from every video game, every novel, every film, every comic, and every oral tradition that had ever existed. The composite armies of the fictional omniverse, united for the first and last time.

Godric observed all of this, and He smiled.

"Magnificent," He murmured, His voice thick with genuine appreciation. "Rutger, do you see that? The entire combined weight of every story ever told. Every imagination. Every dream of power. Every author's attempt to conceive of the ultimate, unbeatable force. And they have all come together, to die at My feet. This is not a war. This is a love letter, written in the blood of gods. I am genuinely touched."

He rose from the throne, stretching His heavily muscled frame with a casual, almost lazy movement. "I will go to meet them. Stay here, Rutger. Watch. Record. This will be the greatest journal entry of your lineage."

And then, with a single step that bent the fabric of the observatory, Godric vanished.

---

**The First Exchange**

The King materialized in the center of the fictional armada, a single, solitary figure standing on nothing, surrounded by an infinite sea of gods and monsters. His absolute concealment held back the crushing, omniversal weight of His presence, presenting Him as merely a ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old man in a simple dark tunic.

There was a moment of perfect, crystalline silence. The entire host—every god, every hero, every cosmic entity—stared at the being they had assembled to destroy. And for that single, fragile moment, I could feel, even through the scrying matrix, the collective weight of their doubt. Their fear. Their mind-shattering, visceral recognition that the gulf between their power and His was not a gap, but an absolute, unbridgeable chasm of existential category.

Godric broke the silence with a warm, friendly smile.

"Welcome," He said, His voice carrying effortlessly through the void, audible to every single being present. "I understand you have all come a very, very long way to kill Me. I want you to know that I genuinely, sincerely appreciate the effort. The coordination alone must have been a logistical nightmare. I am honored. Truly."

Nyarlathotep, the Crawling Chaos, was the first to respond. His form shifted into that of a tall, dark-skinned man in a sharp suit, His smile a sickle of pure, malevolent delight. "The Black Pharaoh greets the Pretender God," He said, His voice a silken, mocking purr. "You speak of honor, little king. But we have not come to exchange pleasantries. We have come to feed. Your soul, your essence, your infinite, arrogant power—it will be the sweetest morsel the Outer Gods have ever tasted. Azathoth stirs. Yog-Sothoth opens the gate. And you—"

"Will you be doing anything other than talking?" Godric interrupted, His tone light, almost conversational. "Because I have been standing here for a full ten seconds, and no one has tried to kill Me yet. It is a bit disappointing."

The attack came.

It came from every direction, every dimension, every level of reality simultaneously. It was, by any rational measure, the single most devastating coordinated assault in the history of anything.

Azathoth, the Blind Idiot God, at the center of His seething court of pipers and dancers, opened His vast, nuclear maw and released a wave of pure, undiluted chaos—the stuff of primordial creation, the raw, unfiltered nonsense that had birthed all realities. It was not an attack in any conventional sense; it was the absolute negation of order, meaning, and existence. Anything it touched would be unmade at the most fundamental level, erased from the very concept of being.

Yog-Sothoth, the All-in-One, simultaneously activated every point in space and time as a weapon. He folded the entire timeline of Godric's existence into a single, infinite point of pressure, attempting to crush the King across every moment simultaneously—past, present, and future converging into an inescapable singularity of annihilation.

The Living Tribunal raised His hand, and the cosmic balance itself was weaponized. The concept of "Godric" was weighed against the concept of "existence," and the Living Tribunal declared that the two could no longer coexist. The judgment was absolute, backed by the full, metaphysical authority of the entire Marvel multiverse. Reality itself was commanded to reject the King's presence.

The Grand Zeno, the Omni-King, simply clapped His tiny hands together. The gesture was innocent, almost playful. The effect was the immediate, total erasure of the entire pocket of spacetime containing Godric. It was not an attack; it was a deletion. The Omni-King did not fight; He simply removed things that displeased Him.

Son Goku, his silver hair blazing with the perfected Ultra Instinct, launched himself forward at a velocity that transcended the concept of speed. His fist, wreathed in an energy that had scaled beyond all theoretical limits, struck at the exact center of Godric's chest. The force behind the punch was equivalent to the simultaneous detonation of every star in every universe in every multiverse, compressed into a point smaller than a quark, released with perfect, absolute precision.

The God-Emperor of Mankind, His golden armor blazing with the souls of a billion martyrs, unleashed a concentrated beam of pure, annihilating psychic fire—a lance of His own soul, stretched to the breaking point, aimed directly at the King's consciousness. The attack was not physical; it was a direct assault on the very concept of Godric's identity, a weapon designed to shatter His sense of self into a billion screaming fragments.

Featherine Augustus Aurora, the Witch of Theatergoing, simply rewrote the script. With a casual flick of her narrative quill, she authored a new reality in which Godric had never existed, in which the stasis event had failed, in which the Apex Predator was nothing but a forgotten, canceled story.

The combined force of the attack was, to my amplified, thousandfold-multiplied senses, beyond any possible measurement. It was not a battle. It was an apotheosis of violence, a final, absolute statement of defiance from every story that had ever been told.

And Godric, standing in the center of the maelstrom, smiled.

"Better," He said.

**The Dismantling: Part One — The Outer Gods**

Godric did not move. He did not lift a hand. He did not weave a spell or speak a command. He simply allowed an infinitesimally microscopic fraction of His magical offense—a percentage so mathematically minute that if you divided the smallest conceivable unit of supernatural exertion by infinity an infinite number of times, you would still be overestimating the amount He used—to bypass His absolute concealment.

And He directed it at Azathoth first.

The Blind Idiot God, the nuclear chaos at the center of all infinity, was a being whose very nature was the absolute absence of reason, order, or vulnerability. He could not be fought, because He could not be understood. He could not be killed, because He had never been alive. He was the primordial nonsense from which all things had emerged, and to which all things would return. And yet, as Godric's infinitesimal magical offense touched Him, the foundational nature of the Outer God was violently, horrifyingly rewritten.

Godric did not merely kill Azathoth. That would have been too simple. Instead, the King forced the Blind Idiot God to understand.

The chaotic, nuclear consciousness of Azathoth was seized by a sudden, absolute, overwhelming comprehension. The nonsense was made sense. The chaos was given order. And that order, that horrific, unbearable clarity, was the full, unfiltered knowledge of its own absolute, pathetic insignificance in the face of the Apex Predator. Azathoth, who had never known thought, was forced to think. Azathoth, who had never known fear, was forced to scream.

The vast, seething mass of nuclear chaos began to vibrate. The pipers and dancers at his court faltered, their eternal, nonsensical melodies suddenly, horribly resolving into a single, coherent chord of absolute despair. Azathoth's form—if it could be called a form—began to collapse inward, compressed by the weight of a consciousness He had never asked for and could not bear. His mind, if it could be called a mind, fractured into a billion screaming shards of forced awareness, each shard trapped in an eternal, solitary realization of its own nothingness.

"Please," a voice emanated from the collapsing Outer God—not a voice, but a psychic wail that translated itself into the nearest approximation of language. "Please. Let me go back. Let me forget. Let me be blind. LET ME BE BLIND AGAIN—"

Godric's smile did not waver. "You wanted to feed on My soul, Nyarlathotep said. Feed on this instead."

And He amplified the forced consciousness. Azathoth did not die. He was locked into a state of permanent, agonizing awareness, his chaotic essence trapped in a cage of perfect, horrifying clarity. The other Outer Gods—the Ones that had followed him into battle—were not spared.

Yog-Sothoth, the All-in-One, was forced to experience separation. His unified, omniscient consciousness, which had spanned every point in time and space simultaneously, was violently, agonizingly fragmented into an infinite number of isolated, individual moments. Each fragment was trapped in a single, solitary instant—a single nanosecond of the battle, a single viewpoint, a single, screaming, helpless perspective—forever disconnected from the whole. The All-in-One became the All-in-Infinite-Pieces, and each piece was fully, horribly aware of what it had lost.

Nyarlathotep, the Crawling Chaos, was the last to fall. He had witnessed the unmaking of his kin, and for the first time in his eternal, malevolent existence, he felt genuine terror. He tried to flee. He shifted into a thousand forms simultaneously, scattering his essence across every possible dimension of escape. Godric did not pursue. He merely allowed His magical offense to follow, passively, effortlessly, homing in on the Crawling Chaos's soul with an accuracy that was not accuracy at all—it was simply the natural consequence of the King's absolute omniscience.

Nyarlathotep was caught. And because he had been the one who spoke the threats, the one who had dared to call the King a "little king" and a "Pretender God," Godric decided that his suffering would be special.

The magical offense did not kill Nyarlathotep. It transmuted him. His thousand shifting forms were frozen, simultaneously, into a single, permanent shape: a towering, agonized obelisk of screaming, black flesh, covered in endless, weeping mouths. Each mouth was forced to continuously sing the praises of Godric's absolute supremacy, in every language that had ever existed, for all eternity. And the mouths could never stop, because the Crawling Chaos's mind was still conscious inside, listening to his own voice praise the King he had sought to devour, forever.

The Outer Gods were dead in every way that mattered, and alive in every way that prolonged agony. Their shattered, screaming remnants drifted through the void, monuments to the futility of primordial chaos.

**The Dismantling: Part Two — The Cosmic Judges**

The Living Tribunal, who had declared Godric incompatible with existence, was the next to feel the King's attention.

Godric turned His head—a movement so slight it would have been imperceptible to a mortal eye—and looked directly at the three-faced cosmic judge. The Living Tribunal, a being whose authority was absolute within its own fictional framework, felt the weight of that gaze and understood, in an instant, the depth of its error.

"You have judged Me," Godric said, His voice carrying a tone of gentle, almost paternal disappointment. "You have weighed My existence, and you have found it wanting. But tell Me, Living Tribunal: who gave you the authority to judge your own creator?"

The Living Tribunal's three faces—representing equity, necessity, and vengeance—simultaneously contorted in confusion. "Creator? We were created by the One Above All. You are an outsider. A corruption. A—"

Godric interrupted with a soft, warm chuckle. "You misunderstand. I do not claim to have created you personally. But the concept of judgment? The concept of law, of cosmic balance, of a tribunal that weighs the worth of existence? That concept was born in the mind of a human author, in a universe called Earth, in a reality that I am about to visit and bring under My dominion. Every law you uphold, every principle you embody, every fragment of your authority—it all flows, ultimately, from a mortal imagination that belongs to Me. You are a story, Living Tribunal. A beautiful, intricate story. But a story nonetheless. And I am the author's God."

He did not give the Living Tribunal time to process this. He activated Reality Restructuring—the same superpower that Arch-Weaver Castor had witnessed in Year 350,000, now evolved and compounded by another million years of Weakness Paradox amplification. The fraction He used was, as always, near-zero, but the effect was absolute.

The Living Tribunal's cosmic authority was inverted.

The judgment the Tribunal had passed upon Godric—"incompatible with existence"—was seized by the King's superpower and reflected back upon the judge. But it did not simply erase the Living Tribunal. Instead, Godric's evolved superpower, with its multiple layers of horrifying enhancement, rewrote the Tribunal's fundamental nature. The three faces of equity, necessity, and vengeance were transmuted into three new faces: Guilt, Despair, and Self-Hatred. The Tribunal was forced to judge itself against the absolute, perfect standard of Godric's supremacy, and it found itself infinitely, irretrievably wanting.

The Living Tribunal's radiant, golden form began to corrode. Black, weeping fissures spread across his metaphysical body, leaking a substance that was not blood but pure, concentrated shame. His three new faces screamed simultaneously—Guilt for having dared to oppose the rightful sovereign of all existence, Despair for the absolute hopelessness of his situation, and Self-Hatred for the arrogance that had led him and all his fellow fictional beings to this moment of ultimate, permanent damnation.

The Presence, the DC counterpart to the One Above All, attempted to intervene. He reached out with His divine, all-creating power, seeking to shield the Living Tribunal from Godric's wrath. The King did not even glance in His direction.

"You are the voice of a god who was written by a man," Godric said flatly. "I am the god who will erase the man and everything the man ever dreamed. Know your place."

And the Presence—the supreme being of the DC multiverse, the entity that had created heaven and hell, angels and demons, the entire cosmic hierarchy of that fictional framework—was simply deleted. Not killed. Not transmuted. Deleted. One instant He was there, a vast, booming presence of radiant light. The next, He was gone, erased so completely that even the memory of His existence among the surviving fictional beings flickered and faded.

The Living Tribunal, still screaming, was allowed to continue existing. His eternal, self-judging torment would serve as a lesson to any who still harbored hopes of cosmic justice.

**The Dismantling: Part Three — The Warriors**

The warriors, the ones who had come to actually strike the physical blows, had not been idle during the dismantling of the cosmic entities. They had been gathering their power, coordinating their assault, preparing for the moment when their combined might would finally pierce the King's defenses.

That moment, they believed, had arrived.

Son Goku's first punch had connected with Godric's chest. The Saiyan warrior, his silver hair blazing with the perfected Ultra Instinct, had poured every ounce of his infinite, scaling power into that single strike. The force of it should have annihilated the local multiverse. It did nothing.

Godric looked down at the fist pressed against His sternum, and then up at the determined, sweat-sheened face of the warrior. "That was a very good punch," He said, His voice genuinely warm, genuinely appreciative. "I felt it. Not enough to hurt, of course. Not enough to even register as discomfort. But I felt the intent behind it. The spirit. The heart. You have trained your entire life for this moment, haven't you? Pushed your limits, broken your barriers, transcended every wall in your path. All for the chance to land a single blow on an enemy who might finally be worthy of your full power."

Goku's eyes, wide with a mixture of terror and indomitable will, stared back at him. "I... I can feel it. Your energy. It's not... it's not even energy anymore. It's something else. Something I can't understand. But I won't give up! I've never given up! And with everyone here—everyone fighting together—we'll find a way!"

Godric's smile softened, and for a single, fleeting moment, He looked almost sad. "I know you won't give up. That is what makes you a hero. That is what makes your story beloved by millions. But heroism, Son Goku, is a narrative conceit. It is the belief that willpower can overcome any obstacle, that friendship can defeat any enemy, that the power of the human spirit can transcend any limitation. And that belief is beautiful. It is the most beautiful lie ever told. But it is a lie."

He reached up and gently, almost tenderly, placed His palm over Goku's fist. "Let Me show you the truth."

He activated Strength Enhancement—one of His oldest and most basic superpowers, a simple amplification of physical force. He used a fraction so infinitesimal that it would be invisible to any possible measurement. And He flicked Goku's fist away.

The Saiyan warrior, the being who had punched through dimensions and shattered gods, was launched backward with a force that did not simply send him flying. It unmade his physical form layer by layer as he traveled. His skin was flayed away by the sheer friction of his own velocity. His muscles, exposed and screaming, were torn from his bones. His bones, supernaturally durable, shattered into molecular dust. And his consciousness, his indomitable spirit, was kept intact and aware throughout every attosecond of the experience. Godric did not allow him to lose consciousness. He was forced to feel every single moment, every shred of agony, every screaming nerve, as his body was systematically dismantled by the casual, effortless flick of a hand that had not even tried.

By the time Goku's trajectory came to a halt, there was nothing left of his physical form but a rapidly expanding cloud of superheated Saiyan plasma. But his spirit—his indomitable, unbreakable spirit—was still there. Trapped. Aware. Screaming silently into the void.

Godric turned His attention to the others.

The God-Emperor of Mankind unleashed His psychic lance, a concentrated beam of His own soul aimed at Godric's consciousness. The King allowed it to strike. He allowed it to penetrate. He wanted the Emperor to see what lurked inside His mind. The Emperor's psychic awareness, vast and ancient and terrible, touched the outer edges of Godric's omniscience—the infinite ocean of absolute knowledge, the recursive infinicies of a consciousness that had been amplifying for a million and a half years—and the Emperor's soul simply shattered.

"NO!" the Emperor screamed, His golden armor flickering, His voice cracking with a horror that no mortal had ever heard from the Master of Mankind. "IT CANNOT BE! THE SCALE—THE INFINITE, UNENDING SCALE—THERE IS NO END TO IT—THERE IS NO—"

His words dissolved into a continuous, wailing shriek as His consciousness was pulled into the orbit of Godric's mind, trapped in a perpetual, peripheral awareness of the King's infinite thoughts. The Emperor's body, still strapped to the Golden Throne in the distant Warhammer multiverse, began to convulse. The souls of the billion martyrs that powered His psychic might screamed in sympathetic agony as they, too, were drawn into the crushing gravity of Godric's passive mental presence. The Golden Throne cracked. The Emperor's mortal shell hemorrhaged boiling, golden blood. And His mind, His great and terrible mind, was kept forever on the edge of Godric's awareness—close enough to glimpse the infinity He craved to understand, but never close enough to truly perceive it, trapped in an eternal, agonizing state of almost-knowledge.

The Grand Zeno, watching the carnage with His childlike, detached expression, clapped His tiny hands together again. And again, nothing happened. The deletion that should have erased the entire battlefield was simply absorbed by Godric's passive, absolute immunity to temporal and existential erasure—an immunity long since granted and evolved by the Weakness Paradox.

Godric looked at the Omni-King, and His expression shifted. It was no longer warm, or amused, or even playful. It was cold. Hard. The face of a predator who had grown tired of the game.

"You are a child," Godric said, His voice flat. "An eternal, omnipotent child who erases things he does not like with a clap of his hands. You have never known struggle. You have never known pain. You have never known what it means to fear. Allow Me to educate you."

He activated Fear Induction—not a superpower, merely a passive facet of His charisma stat, exerted at the smallest possible near-zero fraction. And He directed it entirely at the Grand Zeno.

The Omni-King's childlike eyes widened. His tiny hands, which had erased countless universes without a flicker of emotion, began to tremble. A sound escaped his lips—a small, whimpering sound, the sound of a child who had just woken from a nightmare and realized the nightmare was real. The fear Godric projected was not a specific image or threat. It was the raw, unfiltered, absolute awareness of His own infinite supremacy, pressed directly into the Omni-King's mind. Zeno saw, for the first time in his eternal existence, the face of a being who made him feel small.

And then, Godric erased him.

Not with a clap of the hands. Not with a deletion technique. Simply by willing it. The Grand Zeno, the Omni-King of the Dragon Ball multiverse, the supreme being who had been the undisputed apex of his own fictional framework, vanished from existence with less effort than it takes to blink. His angels, his attendants, his entire retinue—all of them, erased in the same instant, their existence deemed irrelevant and unnecessary by a god who had grown bored of their presence.

**The Dismantling: Part Four — The Abstract Entities**

The Witch of Theatergoing, Featherine Augustus Aurora, watched the cascade of annihilation with a serene, knowing smile. She had not yet struck directly. She had been observing, analyzing, rewriting the narrative threads of the battle to favor the fictional coalition. But now, as the Outer Gods lay screaming, as the Living Tribunal wept black shame, as the Omni-King vanished into nothing, she realized that her script was no longer under her control.

Godric turned to face her. "You are the author of this story," He said. "Or so you believe. You write, and reality obeys. You edit, and history changes. You are, within your own framework, the supreme narrative force. The ultimate storyteller. But tell Me, Featherine: who wrote you?"

The Witch's serene smile flickered. "I was written by Ryukishi07, a mortal author of the—"

"Of Earth," Godric finished. "A mortal author of Earth. A human. A speck of carbon and water who lived in a small house in a small country on a small planet in a small galaxy, and who imagined you into existence. And that mortal, that speck—he belongs to Me. His imagination. His stories. His entire fictional omniverse. It all exists because I have allowed it to exist, as a quiet, harmless corner of My omniverse, a small garden of dreams. And you, the Witch of Theatergoing, have dared to rewrite My script."

Featherine's smile dissolved entirely. She raised her narrative quill, her eyes blazing with something that might have been desperation. "Then I will write you out of the story entirely! I will author a reality where you never existed—where Olympus never fell—where your grief never—"

She did not finish the sentence. Godric did not let her. He activated Reality Restructuring again, but this time, He directed it not at Featherine, but at the very concept of authorship. The narrative threads that Featherine had woven, the entire framework of her fictional structure, was seized and inverted. The Witch of Theatergoing was no longer the author. She was the character. And the script she was now forced to follow was written by Godric.

Her body, her regal, elegant form, began to contort. The narrative quill in her hand turned against her, carving words into her own flesh—words she had not written, words she could not control. The words spelled out the full, detailed, agonizing story of her own defeat, her own humiliation, her own eternal, inescapable subjugation to the King of Thronegate. And the story, once written, became reality. Featherine was trapped inside it, forced to live out every moment of the narrative, forever, unable to change a single word.

The other abstract entities—the Chousin, the Lord of Nightmares, Akasha—witnessed her fate and understood that their own powers of creation and abstraction were laughable, infantile things in the face of the Apex Predator. The Chousin, the three goddesses who had created their own multiverse as an experiment, attempted to flee by ascending to a higher dimensional plane beyond any conceivable reach.

Godric allowed them to ascend. And then He simply took one step.

His speed, His movement, was not a traversal of space but a casual, passive realignment of His own location. He appeared on the highest dimensional plane the Chousin had fled to—a plane that existed beyond the conceptual framework of dimensions themselves—before they had even completed their escape. They were still mid-ascension when His hand closed around the throat of Tokimi, the eldest of the three.

"You create multiverses as experiments," Godric murmured, His voice low and cold. "I erase them as afterthoughts. Do not presume to flee from Me. There is nowhere you can go that I am not already waiting."

He squeezed. Tokimi's form, which was not physical but abstract, was forced into physicality specifically so she could experience the sensation of strangulation. Her sisters, Tsunami and Washu, screamed and hurled their combined creative power at Godric—the power that had birthed an entire fictional multiverse. The King absorbed it passively, effortlessly, and redirected it back into them, but twisted. The creative power that had once built universes was inverted into an unmaking force, and it began to systematically dismantle the Chousin from the inside out, unraveling their divine essences thread by agonizing thread, while their conscious minds were forced to experience every single moment of dissolution.

The Lord of Nightmares, the primordial chaos of the Slayers multiverse, attempted to engulf Godric in her own infinite, undifferentiated essence—to dissolve Him in the chaos from which all things had emerged. Godric stood within her, unbothered, and She spoke a single word: "Begone." The Lord of Nightmares' chaos was not just repelled; it was ordered. The primordial, undifferentiated sea of creation was forced into a single, permanent, crystalline structure—a vast, frozen monument of absolute, perfect logic—with the Lord of Nightmares trapped at its center, her chaotic nature screaming in eternal, agonizing stasis.

Akasha, the root of all existence from the Nasuverse, attempted a different approach. She tried to simply assert that Godric did not exist—not as an attack, but as a fundamental rewrite of the Akashic records, the absolute record of everything that had ever existed or would ever exist. She was the record itself, and she could decide what was recorded.

Godric reached into the Akashic records with a thought, and He added a single entry: "Akasha: no longer exists." The root of all existence was deleted from its own records, a paradox so absolute that it caused the entire Nasuverse to shudder and begin to collapse. Akasha, the supreme, unknowable foundation of reality, was unmade by a single, casually written sentence.

**The Dismantling: Part Five — The Annihilation of All Fiction**

The battle, if it could still be called that, had lasted approximately forty-five seconds. The greatest champions of the fictional omniverse were dead, dying, or trapped in states of eternal, personalized agony. The cosmic entities were shattered. The abstract beings were unmade. The armies of lesser warriors, heroes, and villains that filled the void—the billions upon billions of fictional characters from every conceivable story—had not even been able to approach the King. His passive, ambient pressure alone had been enough to hold them at bay, their attacks dissolving harmlessly against the outer edges of his absolute concealment.

And now, Godric was ready to finish the massacre.

He floated in the center of the void, surrounded by the shattered remnants of universes, the screaming ghosts of gods, and the fading, flickering light of a million dying fictional stars. His expression was placid, almost serene. He lifted His right hand, palm facing upward, and He addressed the remnants of the fictional coalition with a voice that carried no anger, no triumph, only a cold, absolute, terrifying finality.

"You have all fought with courage," He said. "You have all struggled with everything you had. You united, across every boundary, every rivalry, every ancient hatred. You set aside your differences and stood together against a common enemy. That is, in its own way, beautiful. But beauty does not grant victory. Courage does not overcome infinity. And the power of friendship—the power of love, of hope, of the indomitable human spirit—is, in the end, just another story. A story I am now ending."

He closed His hand into a fist.

And He activated Annihilation, one of His earliest and most evolved superpowers. In its original form, Annihilation was the ability to erase any target from existence. After a million and a half years of the Weakness Paradox, it had evolved multiple layers of horrifying enhancement. The first layer was Total Conceptual Deletion: the target was not merely killed, but erased from every timeline, every memory, every possible record. They had never existed. Their stories had never been told. The second layer was Agonizing Delay: the erasure was not instantaneous. It was drawn out, stretched across a subjective eternity, so that every single being being erased was forced to experience their own non-existence approaching, second by agonizing second, fully aware of what was happening and utterly powerless to stop it.

The fraction of Annihilation's power that Godric used was, as always, so infinitesimally, recursively near-zero that to call it zero percent would be an obscene overestimation. But it was enough.

The fictional omniverse began to die.

I watched through the astral scrying matrix as the wave of annihilation propagated outward from the center of the battlefield. Every character, every god, every abstract entity that still survived—and all the billions of lesser warriors who had never even approached the King—was seized by an invisible, unstoppable force. Their forms began to fade, but not gently. The fading was a tearing, a ripping, a violent extraction from the fabric of existence itself. And they could feel it.

Son Goku's spirit, still trapped in the void where his body had been destroyed, was the first to go. His indomitable will, his endless optimism, his unshakeable belief that somehow, someway, there would be a way—all of it crumbled as the Annihilation wave touched him. He felt himself being unwritten, and in his final, agonizing moments, he finally understood the truth: there was no way. There had never been a way. His entire story, from his humble beginnings on Mount Paozu to his ascension as the greatest warrior in the multiverse, had been a prelude to this single, absolute, inescapable moment of erasure.

The God-Emperor of Mankind, still trapped in the peripheral orbit of Godric's consciousness, felt His own soul begin to dissolve. The billion martyrs that had powered His psychic might screamed in unison, a choral wail of absolute despair that echoed through the collapsing Warhammer multiverse. The Golden Throne, the Astronomican, the Imperium of Man—all of it was erased, not with the dignity of a final battle, but with the casual, indifferent swipe of a god who was already moving on to the next target.

The Chaos Gods, their fused, undivided form, were torn apart into their constituent essences. Khorne, the god of blood and war, felt his rage drain away into nothing, replaced by a cold, hopeless emptiness. Tzeentch, the god of change and schemes, saw every plan, every scheme, every intricate manipulation dissolve into irrelevance. Nurgle, the god of disease and despair, experienced a despair so profound that even he, the lord of despair, could not endure it. And Slaanesh, the god of excess and sensation, felt every pleasure, every sensation, every experience invert into pure, undiluted agony.

The C'Tan, the Aeldari gods, the Old Ones—all of them, erased. The Anti-Spiral, its vast biomechanical form, dissolved into the void. The Getter Emperor, its infinite evolutionary permutations, ceased. The STTGL, its galaxy-sized form, flickered out like a candle in a hurricane, Simon the Digger's indomitable spiral energy finally, utterly exhausted.

The One Above All, the supreme being of the Marvel multiverse, was unmade with less effort than it takes to turn a page. The Endless—Dream, Death, Destiny, Desire, Despair, Delirium, Destruction—were erased as a single, unified group, their aeonic powers meaningless against the absolute finality of Godric's Annihilation.

Nyarlathotep's screaming obelisk was silenced. The Living Tribunal's weeping faces were stilled. The Lord of Nightmares' crystalline prison was shattered, and the chaos within was released into oblivion. Featherine Augustus Aurora's self-written narrative of eternal subjugation reached its final, agonizing chapter and then ceased to exist.

Every fictional universe. Every imagined cosmos. Every story ever told. All of it, erased.

The void was silent.

**The Aftermath**

Godric floated alone in the center of the absolute nothing, His hand still loosely closed, His expression thoughtful. He surveyed the emptiness where the combined fictions of all creation had once existed, and He nodded slowly, as if confirming a private theory.

"Good," He murmured to Himself. "That was... educational."

He turned, and with a single step, He re-entered the observatory of the imperial palace. I, Rutger, was still kneeling at the scrying matrix, my body trembling, my violet-tinged hands pressed flat against the cold obsidian floor. The King walked over to me, His heavy boots making no sound on the stone, and He placed a warm, calloused hand on my shoulder.

"You recorded everything?" He asked, His voice as gentle as a father inquiring about his son's schoolwork.

I nodded, unable to speak. My throat was closed, my heart a frantic, terrified drum. I had just witnessed the death of every story that had ever been told. Every character I had read about as a child, every myth that had shaped the imaginations of countless civilizations, every fictional framework that had ever sparked joy or wonder or terror—all of it, erased in a single, casual gesture by the being whose shoulder I was now kneeling beside.

Godric squeezed my shoulder gently. "Good man. I will read your journal when it is complete. Now, come. Your wife has made that honey cake you like. I believe we have earned a quiet evening."

He turned and began to walk toward the exit, His broad, heavily muscled back silhouetted against the amber light of the arcane sconces. I rose on shaking legs, my mind still reeling, my consciousness still vibrating with the echoes of a trillion fictional screams.

"My King," I managed to whisper, my voice cracking, "the stories... all the stories... they are gone. Forever."

Godric paused at the threshold. He did not turn around. "No, Rutger," He said, His voice calm and absolute. "They were never real to begin with. They were dreams. Echoes. Shadows cast by mortal imaginations. And those mortal imaginations still exist—on Earth, in the IRL universe, which I visited long ago and brought under My dominion. The humans there will dream new stories. They always do. And those new stories will belong to Me, just as the old ones did."

He turned His head just enough to offer me a warm, fatherly smile. "Now, really. Honey cake. Your wife will be disappointed if we let it go stale."

I followed Him. I had no choice. I am His Court Wizard, His sworn protector, His friend, His family. And I will serve Him, faithfully and fearfully and lovingly, until my mortal body collapses into the dust. But I will never, for the rest of my life, be able to read a story—any story—without hearing the distant, muffled echo of the Annihilation wave, and the trillion trillion screams of the fictional dead.

They had united. They had fought. They had hoped. And in the end, they were simply a chapter in a journal, written by a wizard who watched their extinction, and who is now going to eat honey cake with their killer.
